lost: Named gratitude as an exclamation rather than a statement — gramercy was something you said before you had composed yourself, the first word of relief or thanks before the formal sentence arrived.
usage: Gramercy, she said, taking both his hands, and he understood that the word was meant to cover more than the umbrella.

definition: An old grey she-cat; a familiar spirit in the form of a cat; a malicious or imperious old woman.
lost: Held the cat and the witch and the imperious elder together in one word — grimalkin understood that all three were variations of the same force.
usage: The grimalkin on the hearth regarded the new arrival with a contempt that seemed wholly considered.

definition: A shudder of horror or cold; the physical sensation of dread; to feel a creeping horror that expresses itself in the body before the mind has caught up.
lost: Named the shudder as an independent event — grue is the body's knowledge arriving before the thought, which is why 'gruesome' kept the root but lost the sensation.
usage: A grue ran through the group when the door opened; nothing was there, but the feeling persisted.

definition: A reward given for service rendered; recompense of a formal or ceremonial kind.
lost: Named reward as something earned and witnessed, not merely given — a guerdon is a public acknowledgment.
usage: He asked no guerdon for the crossing except the name of the man he carried.

definition: Fish offal, blood, and waste from the catch; the debris of the fishing boat's work; the smell and substance of what remains after the valuable has been taken.
lost: Named the residue of labour — gurry is what fishing leaves behind, the evidence of the work that the fish conceals. A boat smells of gurry; a fisherman smells of gurry; it is the signature of the trade that does not wash out.
usage: The quay was slick with gurry by the time the fleet came in, which was how you knew the catch had been good.

definition: A shackle or fetter, especially for the leg; to shackle; to bind at the ankle.
lost: Named the restraint that moved with you — gyves were not a wall or a cell but a weight you carried at every step.
usage: He walked with the careful gait of a man who had worn gyves and had not entirely forgotten the weight.

definition: A cold sea fog rolling inland from the North Sea; a damp coastal mist that arrives without announcement and stays.
lost: Named a specific meteorological event by its feel rather than its physics — haar is not merely fog but the particular cold intimacy of sea air coming ashore.
usage: A haar had settled over the harbour before dawn, and by morning the ships were invisible from the dock.

definition: Originally: the kingfisher, believed to nest on a calm sea; hence: a period of happiness, tranquillity, and perfect weather.
lost: Named the good time by the bird that supposedly caused it — halcyon days were the fourteen days of calm that the kingfisher required for her nest, granted by the gods mid-winter.
usage: A halcyon fortnight in late September that everyone later said had been the last good time before everything changed.

definition: Healthy and vigorous; robust in constitution; free from defect or infirmity — the hale body is the one that has not yet failed.
lost: We kept it in 'hale and hearty' but lost its standalone weight — hale was the medical term before medicine became technical, the word that meant nothing was wrong.
usage: He was hale at seventy in a way that made younger men feel obscurely criticised.

definition: Luck; fortune; what happens to one by chance; the unearned circumstance that shapes a life.
lost: Happy, happen, hapless, mishap — we kept all the derivatives but abandoned the root. Hap is luck understood as simply what falls, before we began to think of luck as something we might influence.
usage: He had arrived here by hap rather than design, which was true of most of the meaningful things in his life.

definition: Perhaps; by chance; if it so happen — a word that holds its uncertainty as a quality rather than an admission.
lost: Perhaps is neutral; haply carries the weight of what hap meant — the acknowledgment that chance governs more than we direct, that perhaps means not just maybe but maybe fate.
usage: Haply she would come, haply not; he prepared for both and tried to keep his face arranged for either.

definition: A knight's complete armour; the full equipment of a man-at-arms; also the gear of a working horse — the word served both.
lost: Named the equipment of war and labour in one word — harness understood that arming a knight and harnessing a horse were structurally the same act, dressing something powerful for use.
usage: He died in his harness, which was how he had always said he wanted to go, and nobody who knew him doubted it.

definition: Divination by the examination of animal entrails — specifically the liver; more broadly, any practice of reading the interior of things for knowledge that the surface does not offer.
lost: We kept the metaphor — "gut feeling" — and lost the word for the practice of taking it seriously.
usage: She had no patience for haruspicy but could not argue with the results.

definition: A coat of chainmail extending to the knees; the principal armour of the medieval knight before plate took over.
lost: Named the garment that made a man into a different kind of object — in a hauberk you moved differently, thought differently, were differently vulnerable.
usage: He had worn the hauberk so long he slept in it toward the end, and the rings left their pattern in his skin.

definition: The space between a ship's bow and her anchor cables; the pipes through which those cables run; the angle and arrangement of cables when a ship is riding to two anchors. A foul hawse is cables twisted around each other — a problem that requires patience or a knife.
lost: Named the ship's most forward anxiety — the hawse is where the vessel connects to the seabed, the point of attachment to something fixed in a world that is not fixed. A clean hawse meant a quiet night; a foul hawse meant work.
usage: The hawse was clear when they checked at midnight; by four the wind had veered and the cables had crossed, and the work of clearing them took until dawn.

definition: The floor of a fireplace; the fire itself as the centre of domestic life; home understood as a place with warmth at its middle.
lost: We kept hearth but lost its weight — it now means home in a sentimental sense, but it once meant the actual fire that cooked the food and heated the room and around which the family organised itself.
usage: The hearth had gone cold before dawn; whoever rose first would have to coax it back, which was understood without being said.

definition: Mental dullness; bluntness of feeling and perception; the state of having been worn smooth.
lost: Named dullness as a condition rather than a trait — hebetude is something that happens to you, not something you are.
usage: The months of paperwork had produced in him a thorough hebetude that no amount of sleep could shift.

definition: A journey made for safety from a dangerous situation; a flight or exodus; departure as survival.
lost: Named the departure that is not a choice — a hegira is forced travel, the journey you make because staying has become impossible.
usage: The family's hegira took three months and three countries and ended somewhere none of them had planned on.

definition: Before this time; up until the present moment — a word that draws a precise line between what existed in all the time before now, and the now that has changed it.
lost: "Previously" is neutral. Heretofore implies the present moment is a rupture — there was a before, and you are in the after.
usage: Nothing heretofore had prepared him for the specific quality of that silence.

definition: Named; called — the archaic passive of 'to call.' Also: to command or summon by name. A person hight so-and-so is a person bearing that name in a world where names still carried weight.
lost: Named naming as a bestowing rather than a labelling — to be hight something was to be called it in a way that meant it, which is a different relationship between person and name than the modern one.
usage: He was hight Ironhand by those who had seen him at the gate, and the name followed him after the gate had been rebuilt.

definition: The remote inland areas beyond a coast or river; territory behind the known frontier; the land that lies beyond the edge of what is mapped.
lost: Named the space beyond the legible — the hinterland is not wilderness exactly but the place where the map's confidence runs out and inference begins.
usage: The hinterland remained officially unmapped for another generation, which meant it was officially inconvenient to govern.

definition: An untranslatable longing for a home — or a version of yourself — that no longer exists and perhaps never did exist quite as remembered.
lost: "Nostalgia" is medical in origin and implies a cure. Hiraeth implies none.
usage: The hiraeth came not for places but for the person she had been in them.

definition: A small, fast falcon used for hawking small birds and insects; a hawk quick enough to take swallows in flight.
lost: We kept hobby only as 'pastime' — the falcon sense, where the word came from, named a precision instrument of the air capable of things larger birds could not attempt.
usage: He kept a hobby for the larks, and a merlin for the skylark, and spent more on their upkeep than on his own.

definition: A formal, public act of allegiance; the ceremony by which a vassal acknowledged a lord's authority.
lost: Named the body's performance of loyalty — homage was not internal but enacted, witnessed, recorded.
usage: He paid homage without irony, which surprised everyone who thought they knew him.

definition: Nevertheless; however that may be; notwithstanding — a concession that holds its ground.
lost: Nevertheless is blunter; howbeit carried a note of philosophical resignation — however things may stand, here is what remains true.
usage: The evidence was thin, howbeit sufficient for the purposes of the court.

definition: A commotion; a loud confused noise; an uproar of voices all speaking at once without resolution.
lost: Named noise at its most social and least musical — hullabaloo is the sound of a group that has lost its ability to take turns, which produces a specific texture of audio chaos distinct from any of its individual components.
usage: The hullabaloo from the street below made concentration impossible and eavesdropping unnecessary.

definition: The middle of the month in the Roman calendar; the fifteenth of March, May, July, and October; the thirteenth of all others — a day of reckoning.
lost: Named the month's midpoint as a fulcrum rather than just a date — the ides was the day everything turned around, and its weight has outlasted its calendar.
usage: He had borrowed the money on the ides and the ides had come again three times without repayment, which had become its own kind of answer.

definition: A will-o'-the-wisp; a phosphorescent light seen over marshes and bogs; a delusive hope or goal — literally 'foolish fire,' the light that leads travellers astray.
lost: Named the light that lies — ignis fatuus is luminescence with malicious intent, or at least with the effect of malice, leading those who follow it away from the path and into the bog.
usage: The project had become an ignis fatuus, drawing more effort and capital each quarter toward a horizon that kept moving.

definition: A spoken curse; the act of calling down evil or harm through deliberate utterance.
lost: Distinguished the curse as a performative speech act from the harm it invokes — an imprecation is a thing you do with your mouth.
usage: She left without violence, but the imprecation she murmured at the threshold was heard by three people.

definition: Official approval; a licence to print a book, originally granted by church authority; the mark that says a thing is permitted to exist.
lost: Named the permission that preceded publication — 'let it be printed' was the phrase, and its withdrawal was censorship. The secular use retains the meaning: official approval granted by whoever has the authority to grant it.
usage: The project had the director's imprimatur, which meant it could be discussed openly, which meant it had already been decided.

definition: Glowing white or brilliantly with heat; emitting light as a result of being heated; also: of performance or emotion: brilliantly vivid.
lost: Named light as a product of heat rather than chemistry — incandescent light is made by making something hot enough to shine, which is a different and more violent relationship between energy and illumination.
usage: The incandescent rage she felt was visible to everyone in the room as a quality of stillness rather than movement.

definition: A series of words chanted or spoken as a magical spell; the use of voice as an instrument of power.
lost: Named speech that does rather than describes — an incantation is language used as action, words that change the state of the world rather than represent it.
usage: The incantation was in a language nobody present understood, which was considered essential to its working.

definition: Of the color of raw flesh; blood-red; to make red as blood.
lost: Held flesh and wound in the same word — incarnadine is the color of both beginnings and ends.
usage: The water was incarnadine by the time they reached the shore, and they understood what that meant.

definition: Embodied in flesh; existing in physical form; an abstraction given a body.
lost: Named the condition of having a body as a specific theological and philosophical state — to be incarnate is to have crossed a threshold.
usage: Standing there in the rain, she seemed to be grief incarnate, stripped of everything incidental.

definition: A contract binding an apprentice to a master for a fixed term; a deed with indented edges, cut in duplicate so each party held a matching piece.
lost: Named the contract by its physical form — the indented cut proved authenticity, the two pieces only matching their original partner. To be indentured was to be bound by a document as well as by a relationship.
usage: He had served his full indenture before anyone realised the master had nothing left to teach him, which had been the case for some time.

definition: To compose or write; to set down in literary form; to dictate the terms of a written text.
lost: Named writing as a compositional act distinct from mere transcription — to indite is to give form, not just to record.
usage: She indited the letter herself, which was unusual, and the handwriting showed it.

definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed in words; beyond the reach of language; the experience that survives the attempt to describe it.
lost: Named the limit of the sayable — ineffable things are not merely difficult to describe but constitutively beyond description, things for which words are structurally inadequate rather than just insufficient.
usage: The view was ineffable in the strict sense — she tried three times on three different postcards and threw them all away.

definition: A corner or alcove beside a large open fireplace; the sheltered recess where the hearth is warmest and the wall cuts the draught.
lost: Named the best seat in the house — the inglenook was where the old sat and the young were sent when they were ill, the warmest corner of the warmest room in a building that was otherwise cold.
usage: He had claimed the inglenook so completely over the years that the chair in it had developed a permanent lean in his direction.

definition: A formal ecclesiastical prohibition; the suspension of religious services from a community as punishment; a ban backed by spiritual authority.
lost: Named the ban that cost you heaven as well as standing — an interdict was not a legal prohibition but a religious one, denying sacraments to an entire community for the sins of its lord.
usage: The interdict had been in force for three years; the children born in that time were unbaptised, which the priest said would be addressed when the bishop lifted it.

definition: To speak or write against with great bitterness; to rail; to pour sustained verbal hostility onto a subject.
lost: Named criticism at the level of sustained attack — inveigh is not a complaint or a critique but a campaign, speech as siege.
usage: He inveighed against the proposal for forty minutes and convinced no one, which he had expected.

definition: A temperature inversion; a layer of warm air trapping cold air below — weather made upside down, the normal relationship between height and temperature reversed.
lost: Named the atmosphere's reversal — in a normal day cold air rises and warm air sinks, but an inversion traps everything, creates fog, traps pollution, makes cities choke. Weather as a failure of the usual order.
usage: An inversion had settled over the valley for three days, and the city below was invisible from the ridge above it.

definition: The act of calling upon a deity, spirit, or authority for assistance; a prayer or formula used to summon a presence or power.
lost: Named the opening gesture of the sacred — an invocation is speech that calls something into the room, that asks a presence to attend, which is a specific and serious use of voice.
usage: The invocation was brief and the gathering, who had expected something longer, shifted slightly in their seats as the silence that followed it settled.

definition: To illuminate; to light up from within or without; to make radiant; to fill with light as if pouring it in.
lost: We kept irradiate but narrowed it to nuclear physics — the word once named any process of light-filling, the way a window irradiates a room in morning.
usage: The low sun irradiated the dust in the air and made the ordinary room look briefly miraculous.

definition: Traveling from place to place without fixed habitation; defined by motion rather than destination.
lost: Named the condition of permanent transit — an itinerant is not between places but has made transit itself the address.
usage: He had been itinerant so long that the question of where he lived produced a small, rehearsed hesitation.

definition: Surely; certainly; indeed — an emphatic affirmation that was heard as 'I wis' and eventually misread as a first-person verb, which is how it vanished.
lost: The word was undone by a misprint — scribes began reading 'iwis' as 'I wis' (I know), and the original meaning was lost in the confusion. A word killed by its own ambiguity.
usage: Iwis, there was never a man more certain of his own error than he was of his own wisdom, which came to the same thing in the end.

definition: Goods thrown overboard from a ship in distress to lighten the load; cargo deliberately sacrificed to save the vessel. Distinguished from flotsam — jetsam was chosen, thrown, abandoned on purpose.
lost: Named the act of deliberate abandonment in emergency — jetsam is what you decide to lose in order to save what remains. The choice between flotsam and jetsam is the choice between loss that happens to you and loss that you choose, which is not a small distinction.
usage: He had shed everything in the last two years — job, flat, most of his friendships — and he was still not sure whether it was flotsam or jetsam, lost or thrown.

definition: A small anchor used to move a ship by hand; to kedge is to carry the anchor out in a boat, drop it, then haul the ship toward it — progress measured in anchor lengths, patience as propulsion.
lost: Named movement achieved by repeated acts of anchoring — to kedge is to move without wind, using the seabed as a grip and human arms as the engine, which is slow and exhausting and sometimes the only option.
usage: They kedged her off the mud over four hours, the men in the boat laying the anchor ahead while those aboard hauled on the capstan.

definition: To wail in lamentation over the dead; a sustained, formal mourning cry; grief given a specific vocal form.
lost: Named grief at its most public — keening is not private weeping but communal, performative mourning, grief as a sound the living make for the dead so the dead know they are missed.
usage: The women keened from the doorway until the cart was out of sight, and then the silence was its own kind of sound.

definition: Knowing; the range of one's knowledge or sight; the distance at which something can be recognised — before it became a poetic compound.
lost: Named knowing as a distance — kenning was originally how far you could see and know, which is a spatial understanding of epistemology that the modern word has entirely abandoned.
usage: Beyond his kenning lay a stretch of coast he had heard described but never seen, and the descriptions had disagreed.

definition: Dry, brittle, withered — of plant matter in late autumn or drought; the quality of things that crackle and break rather than bend.
lost: Named the specific texture of dying vegetation — kexy is the sound and feel of things that have given up moisture entirely, that exist now only as structure without life.
usage: The garden was kexy by August, every stem rattling in the slightest wind, the ground cracking in lines between the beds.

definition: Cows collectively; cattle; the archaic plural of cow, carrying the pastoral weight of a world organized around livestock.
lost: The loss of kine is the loss of a plural that felt like a multitude — cows is a count, kine was a collective presence.
usage: The kine moved across the valley floor in the evening light, slow and certain as weather.

definition: A woman's gown or outer petticoat; a man's tunic reaching to the knees; a garment that defined the body's public shape.
lost: Named a garment that no longer exists — the kirtle was a foundational layer of dress, neither underwear nor outerwear, with no modern equivalent.
usage: She had a kirtle of Lincoln green that she wore to market and kept folded the rest of the year.

definition: One's acquaintances, neighbors, and familiar people; one's home country and its people.
lost: We kept kith only in the phrase kith and kin, which no one says separately — kith alone named the looser circle of belonging, the people who know your name without being family.
usage: He had left his kith so long ago that returning felt less like homecoming than research.

definition: The sound of a bell rung slowly to announce a death or funeral; a sound that announces an ending.
lost: Named the bell's specific funerary voice — a knell is not ringing but tolling, not celebration but announcement, sound as public declaration of loss.
usage: The knell began before they reached the church, which meant the news had arrived before them.

definition: A small gift given to a customer with a purchase; something given or obtained gratuitously; the extra thing that arrives beyond expectation.
lost: Named generosity as a commercial practice — the lagniappe was the baker's dozen extra, the small token that acknowledged the relationship rather than just the transaction, which is a specific understanding of commerce as social act.
usage: The bookseller always tucked a card in with the order — some passage from a book she thought you might not have read — and this lagniappe was why people kept coming back.

definition: Softly bright; playing lightly over a surface without burning; light that moves rather than settles.
lost: Named a quality of light that 'glowing' misses — lambent light is alive, it moves across things rather than emanating from them.
usage: The lambent firelight played across the maps spread on the table.

definition: A pleasant or oppressive physical heaviness; the body slowed to a tempo just below waking.
lost: Named the ambiguous state where exhaustion and pleasure blur — languor is tiredness that might be enjoyable, and that ambiguity mattered.
usage: A summer languor settled over the house from noon until the sea wind came back at five.

definition: The archaic form of lantern, specifically as used aboard ship; the navigation light; also the poop lantern of a ship of the line, which identified the vessel at night and guided the fleet.
lost: The spelling preserves the material — lanthorns were originally made with panels of thinly scraped horn rather than glass, which was expensive and fragile. The horn let light through without shattering in the wind. The word kept the horn long after glass replaced it.
usage: The admiral's lanthorn burned all night, and the fleet steered by it, and when it was extinguished at dawn the lookouts knew to find another reference.

definition: A cool room or cupboard for storing food; originally a room for storing lard and cured meats; the household's food reserve made architectural.
lost: Named the food store by its original contents — a larder was first a place for lard, then for all preserved meats, then for food in general, losing its specific meaning as refrigeration made the cool room unnecessary.
usage: The larder was a small north-facing room that stayed cold through summer, which was its whole purpose and the reason the house had been built facing that way.

definition: The household gods of the ancient Romans; the spirits of dead ancestors believed to protect the family home; the sacred presences that made a house into a home.
lost: Named the domestic divine — lares were the ancestors who had become the house itself, who existed as a protective presence in the walls and the hearth. The phrase 'lares and penates' for household goods keeps the word, but the sacred inhabitants have left.
usage: She kept her lares in a small shelf above the fireplace — photographs, two objects, nothing that needed explanation to someone who understood what the shelf was for.

definition: Physical or mental weariness; exhaustion that goes deeper than sleep can reach.
lost: Distinguished exhaustion from tiredness — lassitude is not solved by rest, which is why it requires its own word.
usage: A lassitude had settled into her bones that she recognized as something other than lack of sleep.

definition: A hiding place; a refuge concealed from the world; any shelter that is also a secret.
lost: Named the hiding place as a specific kind of space — a latibule is not merely concealed but deliberately so, a place you have chosen and fitted to your need for invisibility.
usage: She had a latibule in the library that no one had found in forty years of looking, which says something about how little they looked.

definition: To praise; to extol; to express deep admiration in formal terms. Also: the canonical hour of morning prayer.
lost: We kept laudatory but lost the verb — to laud something was to perform praise, not merely feel it, which is a distinction the modern word has abandoned.
usage: She was lauded at the ceremony with a thoroughness that suggested they had been planning it for some time.

definition: The sheltered side; the side away from the wind; the quarter toward which wind blows and where calm is found. A shore that is a lee shore is one you are being blown toward — comfort turned to danger.
lost: Named shelter as a directional fact — lee is not a place but a relationship, defined entirely by where the wind is coming from, which means it changes every time the wind does.
usage: They worked their way into the lee of the headland and anchored there for the night, the wind nothing but a sound above them.

definition: A physician or healer; one skilled in the medical arts; a doctor before that word carried its current authority.
lost: We kept leech only as the worm — lost the healer, kept the parasite, which says something about how medicine evolved.
usage: The leech came at dawn and spent an hour in silence before pronouncing, unhelpfully, that time would tell.

definition: The sideways drift of a ship caused by wind and current; the distance a vessel is set off her course by forces she cannot control. Hence: the margin of freedom allowed within constraints.
lost: The phrase 'give me leeway' survives; the origin drowns. A ship making leeway is being pushed sideways off her course by wind and tide — she is going where she pointed but also somewhere else, which is an exact description of most human plans.
usage: He needed leeway on the deadline, he said, which was nautically honest — he knew which direction he was meant to go, he just couldn't control what was pushing him sideways.

definition: A young hare in its first year; a hare still carrying the smallness and vulnerability of its first season.
lost: Named age in an animal with a precision we reserved for very few species — the leveret is a hare that hasn't yet become what it will be.
usage: She found a leveret in the long grass, close-huddled and convinced that stillness was invisibility.

definition: A sea monster of immense size; the great beast of the deep; Hobbes's name for the state — the creature that is made of all the people who agree to constitute it.
lost: Named the monster of political philosophy — Hobbes used leviathan because the state he was describing was a creature of human parts assembled into something with its own life and power, larger and more frightening than any of the components.
usage: The Leviathan of the regulatory system had grown so large that nobody inside it could see its own shape.

definition: Gladly, willingly; with equal willingness — used in constructions like 'I had as lief' meaning 'I would just as gladly.' A way of stating preference as equivalence.
lost: Modern English has no clean equivalent for this construction. To say you would 'just as soon' is weaker; 'I had as lief die' cannot be translated without losing something.
usage: "I had as lief stay," he said, which was true, though no one believed him.

definition: One's rightful lord or sovereign; also: bound by feudal loyalty; owing or owed allegiance.
lost: Held both sides of the bond in one word — a liege was simultaneously the one owed loyalty and the one who owed protection.
usage: My liege, he said, and meant it; he had not served the man forty years in order to hedge the word now.

definition: A light, rhythmic musical quality; a rising and falling cadence in voice or music; the sound of something that moves with natural ease.
lost: Named the quality of music and voice that makes listening effortless — a lilt is rhythm you don't notice because it fits so naturally it feels like the shape of the air.
usage: She had a lilt in her voice that made everything she said sound like good news even when it wasn't.

definition: Of or relating to a threshold; occupying a position at or on both sides of a boundary; the state of being between.
lost: Named the threshold as a distinct category of place — liminal spaces are not inside or outside but the place where the transition is happening, which is a specific and disorienting location.
usage: The corridor had the liminal quality of all corridors: it was not where you came from and not where you were going and it did not pretend otherwise.

definition: Lithe and supple; bending easily; having the physical grace of something with no unnecessary stiffness.
lost: Named flexibility as a bodily quality, not merely a metaphor — lissom is about what a body can do without effort.
usage: She moved with a lissom ease through the rigging that made the new hands stare.

definition: A series of petitions recited by a leader with responses from a congregation; any tedious, prolonged recitation — the sacred and the exasperated using the same word.
lost: Named the call-and-response structure of formal prayer — a litany has rhythm, repetition, and a specific relationship between one voice and many, which is both liturgical technique and, eventually, a term for anything that goes on too long.
usage: His complaints had become a litany she could have recited herself, which was how she knew it was time to make a change.

definition: Skill or cunning in the use and arrangement of words; verbal craft; the quality of a writer or speaker who shapes language with the same precision a carpenter gives to wood.
lost: "Wordsmith" is a metaphor of manufacture. Logodaedaly names the specific skill — the cunning, the architecture, the knowing use of the instrument.
usage: His logodaedaly was such that you only noticed, afterward, that you had been led.

definition: A tedious passage; a period of dullness during a journey, performance, or relationship — the stretch where time slows and nothing of consequence happens.
lost: Named the boring part precisely — a longueur is not mere boredom but the boring section of something that has better parts, the dead stretch of an otherwise worthwhile journey.
usage: The third act had longueurs that the audience endured with varying degrees of grace, and the interval was welcomed with more feeling than was usual.

definition: The glow of a lighthouse or lit coastline reflected on low cloud, visible over the horizon before the light source itself can be seen; the ghost of land before land appears.
lost: Named the precursor of arrival — to see the loom of a coast is to know land is there before you can prove it, a knowledge that arrives by inference and light-scatter. Sailors read the loom to navigate in darkness and fog.
usage: They saw the loom of the lightship two hours before they saw the light itself, which was enough to know they had not gone too far north.

definition: Calm, sheltered, and still; protected from the wind; of weather or a place: quiet and undisturbed.
lost: Named shelter not as a structure but as a quality — lown is the calmness that geography provides, the lee side of things.
usage: They found a lown hollow in the hillside and ate their bread there while the gale ran over the top of them.

definition: Glowing with soft light; translucent; emitting a quiet, interior luminescence.
lost: Glowing replaced it but dropped the translucency — lucent things glow from inside.
usage: The stone was lucent in moonlight, as though it had spent the day storing warmth.

definition: A friction match; the first cheap match that could be struck on any rough surface, named for the light-bearer.
lost: Named the match with full awareness of the metaphor — a lucifer was a small private act of creation, fire from nothing, which is either mundane or theological depending on your mood.
usage: He struck a lucifer against the doorframe and held it to the lamp without looking up from the page.

definition: Study or work done by lamplight; laborious, painstaking intellectual work; the product of late-night effort.
lost: Named scholarship by the light it required — lucubration assumes that the important thinking happens at night, by artificial light, at the cost of sleep.
usage: The footnotes bore all the marks of late lucubration: precise, exhaustive, and slightly unhinged.

definition: Weeping; mourning; in a state of expressed grief — not grief contained but grief made visible in the face and the voice.
lost: Named grief at its most legible — lugent is not sorrow kept private but sorrow that shows, that cannot be composed, that has taken over the face and the posture.
usage: The lugent faces at the station told the new arrivals what the telegraph had not yet carried to their town.

definition: Originally: pale yellow, ghastly, wan — the color of a bruise or a poisoned face; later: garish, sensational; the word traveled from pallor to glare.
lost: One of the most complete meaning reversals in English — lurid first meant the wan yellow of something dying or diseased, then came to mean garish brightness. The original sense is more useful and more frightening.
usage: A lurid cast to his skin that the others noticed but did not mention, waiting to see if it resolved on its own.

definition: A strongly alkaline solution leached from wood ash; used for making soap and for cleaning; the domestic chemical that made cleanliness possible before industrial manufacture.
lost: Named the substance that enabled hygiene before chemistry named its ingredients — lye was made from fireplace ash soaked in water, which means the fire that heated the house also provided the means to wash it.
usage: She made the soap from lye and tallow as her mother had taught her, which took two days and produced a result that cleaned better than anything the shop sold.

definition: Presumptuously bold; impudent in a way that exceeds one's station; forward without warrant.
lost: Named the specific social crime of speaking above yourself — malapert is impudence calibrated to hierarchy, a word that required a class system to mean anything.
usage: The boy was malapert in a way his father had been too, and the resemblance was not flattering.

definition: A spoken curse; the utterance of evil wishes against a person; the formal opposite of a benediction.
lost: Named the curse as speech act, the deliberate opposite of blessing — a malediction had the same structure as a prayer but inverted, which gave it a specific kind of weight.
usage: The malediction was delivered quietly, which was worse than shouting, because it had clearly been considered.

definition: A curse; a formal invocation of evil upon a person; the opposite of a benison.
lost: Named the curse as a performed speech act with moral weight — a malison was not an oath but a prayer directed downward.
usage: She left without violence, but the malison she whispered at the gate was heard by enough people that it traveled.

definition: A military engine for hurling large stones; a siege weapon that converted torsion or counterweight into projectile force; the machine that made stone walls negotiable.
lost: Named the technology that changed what architecture meant — a mangon made walls temporary things, subject to sufficient patience and the right lever arm. The word is gone but the engineering principle remains in every modern weapon.
usage: They brought up three mangons on the third day, which told the castellan that the besiegers had decided on a longer timeline than he had hoped.

definition: To free from slavery; to release from bondage by formal legal act.
lost: Named liberation as a specific legal performance — to manumit was to undo a legal act with another legal act, which made the freedom real in the same register as the captivity.
usage: He manumitted his last remaining bond-servant on the day the law made the gesture redundant.

definition: The borderlands between two territories; the disputed or lawless zone where one sovereignty ends and another is uncertain; land defined by its adjacency to elsewhere.
lost: Named the border as a territory in its own right — the marches were not a line but a region, a zone of ambiguous jurisdiction with its own culture of accommodation and violence.
usage: In the marches the law of neither kingdom ran with confidence, which suited some residents better than others.

definition: A person who ruins a plan by meddling; one who spoils a scheme through interfering at the wrong moment.
lost: Named the ruiner by what they do to plans — a marplot is not an enemy but someone on your own side who intervenes at exactly the wrong moment and with exactly the wrong information.
usage: There was always a marplot in these operations — someone with good intentions and poor timing who undid in minutes what had taken weeks to arrange.

definition: In spite of; notwithstanding; despite — used to acknowledge an obstacle and announce that it does not matter.
lost: Despite is apologetic; maugre is defiant. The word acknowledged the opposition directly and dismissed it in the same breath, which is a different rhetorical act.
usage: Maugre the weather and the road and the advice of everyone who knew anything about the matter, they went anyway, and arrived.

definition: Perhaps; it may happen that — a cousin to peradventure, lighter in register, closer to everyday speech.
lost: Perhaps arrived from French and pushed out the native stock — mayhap, maybe, mayhaps, haply, peradventure all named possibility with slightly different weights before perhaps consolidated everything into one neutral word.
usage: Mayhap she'll come, mayhap she won't, he said, which was either wisdom or evasion and served equally well as both.

definition: To follow a winding course without clear direction; from the River Meander in Asia Minor, famous for its curves.
lost: Named wandering after a river — meander is travel that follows the landscape rather than crossing it, movement shaped by what it moves through.
usage: The path meandered along the valley floor in a way that suggested it had been made by cattle rather than engineers.

definition: Low spirits; depression without spectacular cause; a heaviness that settles in without event.
lost: Named the condition of being simply, dully low — megrims has no drama, which is precisely the point.
usage: He could not explain the megrims to anyone; it seemed too small a thing to name and too large to carry.

definition: A singer; one who creates or performs melodies; a composer of song.
lost: Named the singer as an artist rather than a technician — a melodist makes melody, which is a different claim than merely producing it.
usage: The melodist they'd hired for the evening had a voice that made the room go quiet in a way that felt involuntary.

definition: The smallest European falcon; a fierce, fast hawk used for hunting skylarks; a bird of speed and precision disproportionate to its size.
lost: Named one of the most accomplished hunters in English skies — the merlin's speed made it the specialist's bird, the falcon for those who understood what small and fast could do.
usage: A merlin came off the hillside and took a lark in full flight, which was over before you understood what you had seen.

definition: Fitting; exactly appropriate; measured to the precise degree required and no more.
lost: We kept meet in 'meet and proper' but abandoned mete as a standalone — the word named appropriateness as a kind of precision, fitness understood as measurement.
usage: The punishment was mete; no one argued that it wasn't, which was almost worse than if they had.

definition: A refuse heap; a domestic rubbish dump; the accumulated waste of a household or settlement that archaeologists later read like a text.
lost: Named the place where things were put when they were finished — a midden is a household's autobiography in broken pottery and food bones, which is why archaeologists love them.
usage: The midden at the edge of the settlement had been growing for a generation and had become, by now, a kind of landmark.

definition: Thick, impenetrable darkness; a blackness that has weight and texture.
lost: Modern English lost a word for darkness that felt physical — dark tells you about light, mirk tells you about what you're walking through.
usage: The mirk of the close swallowed him before he reached the second door.

definition: To displease; to offend; to fail to please in a way that carries some moral weight — not merely to disappoint but to fall short of what was owed.
lost: Named displeasure as a relational failure — to misqueme someone was to fail them in a specific way, to produce in them a response that your actions had not been adequate to prevent.
usage: If I misqueme you in this, I ask only that you say so plainly, which would be more useful than what I suspect you will do instead.

definition: Very fine drizzling rain; to rain in very fine drops; weather that is technically dry and practically wet.
lost: Names the exact register between fog and rain that English normally has to describe with a phrase — mizzle is weather you can almost argue about.
usage: It had been mizzling all afternoon, not enough to justify shelter but enough to soak you if you stayed out.

definition: A mole; literally the earth-thrower; the creature that inverts the ground as casually as turning a page.
lost: Named the mole by what it does rather than what it is — moldwarp understands the animal as verb, as action, as geological force at small scale.
usage: The lawn was riddled with moldwarp hills by morning, each one a small, perfect uprising.

definition: Wandering over hills and mountains; one who roams the high ground by preference rather than necessity.
lost: Named the upland wanderer by their terrain rather than their motion — a montivagant is defined by where they go, and where they go is high, which implies a relationship to altitude that the word encodes without explaining.
usage: He had been montivagant since youth and regarded valleys with the mild suspicion of someone who prefers to see what is coming.

definition: In the process of dying; in terminal decline; no longer capable of development.
lost: Applied to institutions as well as bodies — moribund named the long slow end that dying doesn't quite capture.
usage: The tradition was moribund before anyone admitted it; they kept celebrating it out of a shared reluctance to say so.

definition: The following day; the morning that comes after; the near future when it is still close enough to touch.
lost: Tomorrow tells you when; morrow tells you how close — the word had an intimacy that tomorrow, with its extra syllable, slightly refuses.
usage: On the morrow they would go; tonight they ate and did not speak of it.

definition: Of crops or hay, spoiled by becoming overheated in the stack; damaged not by fire from outside but by the slow internal combustion of stored vegetation.
lost: Named a specific agricultural disaster — mowburnt hay was ruined from within, by its own heat and moisture, which made it a loss that was hard to see coming and impossible to stop once begun.
usage: Half the winter fodder was mowburnt by October, which meant the calculation about the livestock had to be made earlier than anyone wanted.

definition: Wandering throughout the world; one who travels not toward anything but continuously, treating the earth itself as the itinerary rather than the route.
lost: "World traveler" implies a home to return to. Mundivagant implies the traveling is the home.
usage: The mundivagant gave no fixed address and required none.

definition: Thick darkness and obscuring mist together; weather that is both dark and obscured; the compound failure of light and visibility.
lost: Darker than fog, colder than dark — murk is the specific atmospheric condition in which you cannot see and cannot trust what you cannot see.
usage: They waited for the murk to lift, and when it didn't they went anyway, which was when the trouble started.

definition: In the dark; blindly; groping through darkness without a light and without certainty of what the darkness contains.
lost: Named moving in darkness as a specific mode of travel — murklins is darkness experienced from inside it, the dark as a medium you move through rather than a condition you observe.
usage: She went murklins to the kitchen at three in the morning and found, by the sound it made, that the cat had knocked the good glasses from the shelf.

definition: A continuous low murmuring sound; also: a flock of starlings in collective, wheeling flight.
lost: Named two things that share a quality — the flock and the sound both move in patterns too complex to track individually.
usage: The murmuration turned above the estuary as the light failed, and no one said anything.

definition: A disease afflicting cattle or other animals; a plague of livestock; any epidemic that moves through a herd.
lost: Named the disaster that preceded famine in an agricultural world — a murrain did not just kill animals but undid the economic structure that kept people fed.
usage: The murrain had taken half the parish's cattle before anyone understood what was happening.

definition: A confused, hand-to-hand fight involving many combatants; the breakdown of formation into individual struggle.
lost: Named the moment when battle stops being tactical and becomes personal — the mêlée is war at its most intimate and most chaotic.
usage: Once the lines broke it was a mêlée, and in a mêlée training matters less than size and fury.

definition: Nevertheless; notwithstanding; none the less — but with more weight, as if the speaker is physically pushing against what has gone before.
lost: Nevertheless is a long word doing a modest job; natheless is a short word doing the same job with more force. The length difference reverses the register of the modern word.
usage: The evidence was entirely against the proposition; natheless, the jury returned for the defendant, and no one who knew the defendant was surprised.

definition: To chatter or grumble; to talk at length about nothing in particular; also: to find fault in a low, persistent tone.
lost: Named the register of idle talk that has a slight edge — natter is conversation that is both companionable and mildly critical, which is a very specific social mode.
usage: They nattered over the fence for half an hour about nothing that mattered and felt considerably better for it.

definition: The tide at its lowest range; the lesser tide that occurs between spring tides when sun and moon pull at angles to each other rather than together.
lost: Named the tide's restraint — a neap tide is the sea withholding, neither fully in nor fully out, the moon and sun in disagreement. To be neaped was to be stranded in harbour by insufficient water, which was its own kind of fate.
usage: They were neaped for three days, the water never quite high enough to float her off the bar, which gave them time they had not wanted.

definition: Communication with the dead as a means of divination; the use of the dead to reveal what the living cannot know.
lost: Named the specific epistemological claim that the dead know more than the living — necromancy assumed that death was an expansion of knowledge rather than its termination.
usage: The charge was necromancy, which was serious enough that the trial was held in camera and the records afterward sealed.

definition: Soft, tender; weak or vulnerable to cold; having flesh that feels too much.
lost: Named the specific vulnerability of a body not hardened to its climate — nesh has no modern equivalent because we stopped valuing the distinction.
usage: She was nesh in a way that made northern winters a sustained personal insult.

definition: Lower; situated beneath; belonging to the regions below — the nether world is the world under the world.
lost: We kept nether in nether regions as euphemism, which stripped it of its actual weight — nether originally described genuine depth, the places beneath the places you can see.
usage: The nether rooms of the building had not been entered in years, and what was stored there had become unclear.

definition: A luminous cloud or halo surrounding a deity or sacred person; also: a rain cloud dark with contained light.
lost: Named the cloud that carries light on the outside while holding rain within — nimbus held the paradox of the luminous storm, which is why meteorology borrowed it for the specific cloud type.
usage: A nimbus of cigarette smoke hung around him in the lamplight, which made him look less divine than he intended.

definition: Bitterly, piercingly cold; of a wind: penetrating and savage; cold that feels like a personal affront.
lost: Cold tells you about temperature; nithering tells you about character — a nithering wind has intent, it finds gaps, it does not merely chill but pursues.
usage: A nithering wind off the moor had turned the afternoon hostile, and the walkers descended an hour earlier than planned.

definition: A coward; a person who has broken the bonds of honor and community; a wretch who has placed themselves outside the social order by their baseness.
lost: Named the coward as an outcast rather than a type — a nithing was not merely afraid but formally excluded, named in public, stripped of the protection that belonging confers.
usage: He was called nithing in the assembly, and after that he had no standing to object to anything that was done to him.

definition: The obligation of honourable, generous behaviour associated with high rank or birth; the inescapable duty that privilege creates.
lost: Named the binding condition of inherited advantage — noblesse oblige is the claim that power comes with obligation, that what you were born to requires something of you. Its irony is that those who most need the reminder are least likely to apply it.
usage: He performed his noblesse oblige with the thoroughness of a man who understood it as a transaction rather than a virtue.

definition: A bioluminescent marine organism; the night-light of the sea; the source of the phosphorescent glow sometimes seen in disturbed seawater.
lost: Named the sea's own light — noctiluca is the water itself made luminous, the ocean remembering that light comes from life as well as from the sky.
usage: The wake behind the boat trailed noctiluca, the water glowing cold blue wherever the hull had broken it.

definition: Shining in or by night; specifically of the high, faint clouds that catch the sun after it has set for everyone below and glow alone against full dark.
lost: We have no common word for this. The phenomenon kept its name only in the scientific register, where it lost all strangeness.
usage: The noctilucent clouds were the only witnesses to what happened on the ridge.

definition: Wandering by night; given to nocturnal roaming.
lost: Named a practice that streetlights and risk aversion have nearly extinguished — the night had its own kind of traveller.
usage: She was by nature noctivagant, more alive at midnight than at noon.

definition: A record or journal kept specifically of things observed, thought, or done during the night-hours. A chronicle of what the day refuses to hold.
lost: A diary is for days. Noctuary acknowledges that the night requires its own accounting.
usage: What the noctuary contained, he never shared with morning company.

definition: The present time or occasion; the particular moment in question — 'for the nonce' meaning for now, for this specific occasion, without commitment to any other time.
lost: Named the moment without extending it — the nonce is this occasion only, with no claim on what comes next. A nonce word is a word made for one specific use and not kept afterward, which is the purest expression of what the word means.
usage: For the nonce, they agreed to disagree, which meant the disagreement was simply rescheduled rather than resolved.

definition: The middle of the day; noon understood as a tide — a high point from which the day will ebb in either direction.
lost: Noon is a number; noontide is a moment with properties — the word understands midday as a turning point rather than merely a position on the clock.
usage: At noontide the market was at its fullest and the light was directly overhead and flattering to nothing.

definition: A thing as it is in itself, independent of perception; the object beyond all possible experience — Kant's name for what we cannot know but cannot stop trying to.
lost: Named the unknowable thing that generates the knowable appearance — the noumenon is what is there when nobody is looking, which we cannot know because looking is all we have.
usage: He had spent forty years in philosophy and arrived at the conviction that the noumenon was not a limitation of knowledge but knowledge's most honest description of itself.

definition: The entrails of a deer, especially as prepared for eating; the humble offal served to those below the lord's table.
lost: The source of 'humble pie' — umble pie was numble pie, the offal dish given to servants. The humility was literal, edible, and deliberately staged.
usage: The numbles were set at the lower end of the table and eaten without comment by those who understood their position.

definition: Filled with a sense of the divine or supernatural; having a quality of sacred awe that borders on dread. The feeling of standing in the presence of something vast.
lost: "Spiritual" is comfortable. Numinous retains the original terror of the encounter.
usage: The clearing was numinous in a way that discouraged investigation.

definition: Strong public condemnation; the state of being talked about with contempt; verbal abuse from many directions at once.
lost: Named condemnation as a condition you inhabit rather than receive — obloquy is what surrounds a person when the community has turned its mouth against them.
usage: She had survived the obloquy and come out the other side somewhat harder and considerably less interested in other people's opinions.

definition: The part of the visible sea beyond the harbour or coastal waters; the deep water a ship must reach before setting a course. In the offing: near enough to be seen, far enough not yet to have arrived.
lost: The phrase survived and the origin drowned — 'in the offing' means imminent, but the offing was a specific zone of water, the visible horizon of the deep sea, where ships appeared before they entered port. The imminence was literal first.
usage: A sail in the offing that none of them could identify, holding its distance through the afternoon until the light failed.

definition: An event regarded as a portent of good or evil; a sign read as a message from the future; the world speaking in code.
lost: Survived but diminished — omen now implies superstitution, a mild irony. In its original sense it was a serious category of evidence, the world offering information through coincidence and pattern to those trained to read it.
usage: He was not a superstitious man, but the omen was so exact in its application that he found himself taking the longer road anyway.

definition: Of or relating to dreams; having the quality of a dream — not merely surreal but specifically possessing the logic, the certainty, the categorical shifts that belong only to the dreaming mind.
lost: "Dream-like" is a comparison. Oneiric is a quality — a condition of the object itself, independent of any observer's state.
usage: The whole morning had an oneiric quality; she checked, twice, that she was actually awake.

definition: Harsh public criticism; the disgrace attached to shameful conduct; a cause of shame — both the judgment and the condition it produces.
lost: Named disgrace as a social verdict rather than a private feeling — opprobrium is what others say about you, which can outlast the act that caused it and attach to a name permanently.
usage: The opprobrium attached to his name was still in circulation decades after the event, having by then entirely detached from any specific memory of what he had done.

definition: A priest or priestess through whom a deity speaks; the response given; any person or thing regarded as an infallible source of wisdom.
lost: Named the medium and the message in one word — an oracle is both the mouth and what the mouth says, the institution and its output, which is a useful conflation.
usage: She had become the oracle of the office in the way that competent people do: by being right often enough that being consulted became a habit.

definition: A prayer; especially a private or personal prayer — not the liturgical form but the individual voice addressing the divine.
lost: Prayer is the act; orison was the word — a prayer is a practice, but an orison is a specific utterance, one prayer said once, which is a more intimate and particular thing.
usage: She said her orisons in the dark, quickly and without theatre, which seemed to her the more honest way.

definition: To harden into bone; to become rigid and incapable of change; to lose flexibility permanently.
lost: Applied to institutions and minds as well as bodies — ossify names a process that is both biological and moral.
usage: The policy had ossified over decades until no one could remember why it had been written.

definition: A vessel or chamber for the bones of the dead — not a tomb, which houses the body; an ossuary is what remains after the body has been superseded by the skeleton, the last record of a life.
lost: "Burial place" is a destination. Ossuary names the function — the keeping and containing of what remains after everything else has left.
usage: The ossuary was small and very old, predating the church that had eventually been built around it.

definition: In or to some other place; elsewhere — but carrying a suggestion of unreachability, of a distance that is not merely physical.
lost: "Elsewhere" is a direction. Otherwhere is a state of permanent displacement.
usage: Her thoughts were otherwhere long before her body followed.

definition: A blackbird; also the ring ouzel, a mountain thrush with a white crescent on its breast; any dark thrush of the old world.
lost: Blackbird displaced ousel without improving on it — ousel had the advantage of not describing what you were seeing but simply naming it, which birds deserve.
usage: An ousel had been singing from the same holly tree every morning for a week, which she took as a form of ownership.

definition: To flow over the brim; to spill beyond the rim of a vessel; to exceed the containing limit.
lost: Named overflow as a specific moment — the instant the surface breaks its boundary. Spill describes the aftermath; overbrim names the threshold.
usage: The river had overbrimmed its banks in the night and was now conducting its own survey of the lower fields.

definition: A toad or frog; the amphibian in its older name, before paddock narrowed to mean only an enclosed field.
lost: The word survived but was evicted from its original meaning — a paddock in a field is now a piece of land, but it was once the creature that lived in the damp corners of it.
usage: She would not have a paddock killed; she said they kept the garden honest, and no one argued.

definition: A song of praise or exultation; a joyful expression of thanks or triumph; originally a hymn to Apollo.
lost: Named praise at its most musical and communal — a paean is not one person complimenting another but a collective utterance of joy, praise become a sound the room makes together.
usage: The review was a paean to the production and somewhat less useful than something more mixed would have been.

definition: Prolonged, idle, and usually flattering talk; elaborate verbal ceremony that delays the real business indefinitely.
lost: Named negotiation that has lost its purpose — palavering is the form of discussion without the function, talk as performance.
usage: After an hour of palavering they had agreed on nothing except to meet again, which everyone understood was its own kind of answer.

definition: An area within determined bounds or subject to a particular jurisdiction; the fence or boundary that marks what is governed.
lost: The phrase 'beyond the pale' kept the concept alive but removed the geography — the pale was a real administrative boundary, and crossing it meant leaving law itself.
usage: Beyond the pale there were no sheriffs and no assizes; there was only what you could hold.

definition: A horse with a smooth ambling gait, especially one suitable for a lady or for gentle riding; refinement in horse form.
lost: Named the horse by the quality of its movement — a palfrey's amble was a social accomplishment, a gait bred to make riding effortless and the rider graceful.
usage: She rode a grey palfrey that moved so smoothly she could read as she travelled, which was considered either admirable or alarming depending on who observed it.

definition: A fence of pointed stakes forming a defensive barrier; the simplest fortification; what you build when you have wood and time and no stone.
lost: Named the first line of defence — a palisade is fortification at its most honest, a line that says this far and no further, made of whatever the forest provided.
usage: They had the palisade up by nightfall, which was not enough to stop a determined assault but enough to make it cost something.

definition: A dark cloth draped over a coffin; anything that covers and darkens; a thick cloud of smoke or shadow that falls over a landscape.
lost: Named covering as a funerary act — a pall is not merely a shadow but a preparation for burial, which is why the phrase 'cast a pall' carries the weight it does.
usage: A pall of smoke hung over the valley from fires they could not see, which suggested a scale of burning nobody wanted to confirm.

definition: A straw mattress; the bed of servants and the poor; sleep on something that rustles and smells of the field.
lost: Named the bed that most people slept on for most of history — not a mattress in the modern sense but a sack of straw that compacted and had to be restuffed, that harboured things, that marked your station.
usage: He woke on the palliasse with the impression of straw on his cheek and the smell of it in his hair and felt, briefly, that he had gone back twenty years.

definition: An unnatural paleness; the specific whiteness of a face when something is wrong.
lost: Named paleness as a diagnostic — pallor is not a complexion but a signal, and the distinction is what makes it useful.
usage: The pallor of his hands when he set the letter down told her everything before he spoke.

definition: A sudden, sharp pain — physical or emotional; a quick, piercing sensation that arrives without warning.
lost: Named the quality of sudden-onset pain: its brevity and its completeness — a pang is over almost before you register it.
usage: A pang of something — guilt, or its near neighbor — crossed his face before he looked away.

definition: A conference between opposing sides in a conflict; a formal discussion under truce; the moment when fighting stops to see if talking might work.
lost: Named the pause in violence as a formal institution — a parley had rules, flags, and an understanding that killing was suspended for the duration.
usage: They sent a herald to request a parley, which the commander granted, which told him something about the state of their supplies.

definition: A pagan; a non-Christian, especially a Saracen or Muslim in the context of crusade literature; the heathen other.
lost: Named the theological outsider with a word that acknowledged a world divided by faith rather than nation — paynim is a category of belief, not of race.
usage: The romances were full of paynims who fought honorably and believed wrongly, which was understood to be a tragedy.

definition: A loud, prolonged ringing of bells; a series of changes rung on a set of bells; any loud, sustained sound of joy.
lost: Named the bell at its most joyful and complex — a peal is bells doing something together that no single bell can do, sound as collective achievement.
usage: The peal from the cathedral carried across the city and told everyone who could hear it that something had been decided.

definition: Sinning; guilty of moral offense; also: causing disease, as a peccant humor in the body.
lost: Held sin and sickness as the same structural disorder — peccant understood that the body and the soul could both go wrong in the same way.
usage: He listed his peccant hours with a precision that suggested he'd been keeping count for some time.

definition: A long, narrow flag tapering to a point, flown from a lance; the personal standard of a knight below the rank of banneret.
lost: Named the flag that identified you in battle — in the mêlée a pennon was how your side knew where you were, and the enemy knew whom to target.
usage: His pennon was still flying when the rest of the field had broken, which was either courage or obstinacy and possibly both.

definition: The partial shadow at the edge of the total shadow; the zone between full light and full dark; the threshold of the umbra.
lost: Named the margin between extremes — the penumbra is not darkness or light but the precise zone of transition, which has its own character and its own quality distinct from either end.
usage: He existed in the penumbra of the scandal — not implicated but adjacent, not guilty but not entirely untouched.

definition: Perhaps; possibly; by chance — a modal word that holds a door open without walking through it.
lost: Maybe is too casual; perhaps is too neutral; peradventure carried the weight of genuine uncertainty, as if the speaker understood what was at stake in not knowing.
usage: He had not come, and peradventure would not come, and she began to make her arrangements accordingly.

definition: To walk through or around an area; to travel through on foot; to survey a boundary by walking it — the official inspection that becomes the casual stroll.
lost: Named walking as a survey — to perambulate was originally to walk a boundary in order to know it, which gave walking the status of a legal and administrative act. The casual perambulation kept the word while losing the purpose.
usage: He perambulated the estate each morning in a circuit that had not varied in thirty years, which told you something about both the estate and him.

definition: To travel, especially on foot over a long distance; to journey through foreign places.
lost: Named long walking as a purposeful act — to peregrinate is to travel as a practice, not just a displacement.
usage: He had peregrinated through four countries before settling, briefly, in a village that forgot him almost immediately.

definition: The act of travelling through foreign places; a journey, especially a long and wandering one through unfamiliar territory.
lost: Named long travel as its own category of experience — a peregrination is not a trip or a journey but an extended engagement with foreign ground, travel that goes on long enough to change the traveller.
usage: His peregrination through four countries over two years had produced a manuscript and a reluctance to sleep indoors that took another year to resolve.

definition: Light, teasing, deflective talk; banter designed to avoid engagement while appearing sociable.
lost: Named the conversational mode that looks like wit but is actually evasion — persiflage is the opposite of rede.
usage: She met every serious question with persiflage so fluid that you only realized the deflection after she'd left the room.

definition: Anxiety; mental unease; disturbance of the mind caused by uncertainty or concern — also: a deviation in a system caused by an external force.
lost: Named anxiety as a disturbance — perturbation is the word physics uses for the way a system is thrown off its regular motion by an outside force, and also the word for what that force does to a person's peace of mind. Both uses are honest.
usage: He concealed his perturbation well enough that only people who knew him well saw it, and only then in the way he held his hands.

definition: The scent of rain on dry earth; the distinctive smell produced when rain falls on soil that has been dry; the olfactory signal of precipitation arriving.
lost: Coined in 1964 from Greek — petros (stone) and ichor (the fluid that flows in the veins of the gods) — but named something ancient and familiar. That a smell this universal had to wait until 1964 for a word suggests how long we spent not attending to it.
usage: The petrichor reached them before the rain did, and they had just enough time to bring the chairs in.

definition: A grey alloy of tin with small amounts of copper and antimony; the everyday tableware of the medieval household — not silver, not clay, but the middle register of domestic dignity.
lost: Named the metal that democratised the table — pewter was what households used when they couldn't afford silver but had risen above wooden trenchers. Its dull grey surface held the fingerprints of everyone who had used it, which is its own kind of record.
usage: The pewter had been in the family longer than anything else, and its history showed in the worn spots where three generations of thumbs had held it.

definition: An illusion; a ghost; a mental image of something not present — the thing seen by the mind rather than the eye.
lost: Named the image that arrives without an object — a phantasm is a perception without a source, vision producing what isn't there, which is the structure of both hallucination and memory.
usage: The face was a phantasm she could not shake — appearing in mirrors, in windows, in the faces of strangers who did not know they were substituting for someone else.

definition: A bitter, sustained verbal attack; a speech of denunciation.
lost: Named the register of formal verbal assault — a philippic is not an outburst but a prepared instrument.
usage: He delivered his philippic calmly, which made it considerably worse to receive.

definition: The vertical groove between the nose and upper lip; the specific indentation of the human face that has no modern vernacular name.
lost: One of the few body parts that never acquired a common name — the philtrum has only its technical term, which most people don't know, which means there is a feature of every human face that cannot be pointed to with a common word.
usage: She had a deep philtrum that made her look, at rest, as if she were about to say something important, which was either a gift or a burden depending on the occasion.

definition: Light emitted by a substance without combustion; a cold, sustained glow; luminescence that continues after the exciting source has been removed.
lost: Named light that persists beyond its cause — phosphorescence is what glows after the energy has passed, light stored and slowly released, which is a different kind of luminosity from fire or electricity.
usage: The phosphorescence in the wave crests made the night crossing beautiful in a way that was difficult to describe accurately afterward.

definition: A journey to a sacred place as an act of devotion; travel with a destination that is also a purpose; movement that is already an arrival.
lost: Named the journey that is also the practice — a pilgrimage is not travel toward something sacred but travel that is itself sacred, the walking as meaningful as the destination, which is either the most serious or the most honest approach to distance.
usage: The pilgrimage took six weeks and she arrived at the shrine with blisters and a clarity about certain things that she had not expected the walking to produce.

definition: To waste away with longing, grief, or deprivation; to decline slowly through the body's failure to sustain what the spirit requires.
lost: We kept pine as a noun and a tree and lost the verb — the waste of a person declining not from illness but from want of something that cannot be replaced.
usage: She pined for the first year so visibly that everyone remarked on it; by the second year she had become more private about it.

definition: A light, gentle puff or breath of wind; the faintest movement of air on a still day.
lost: Named wind at its minimum — pirr is wind that barely justifies the word, a movement of air that you feel only because everything else is still.
usage: Not a breath of wind all morning, then a pirr across the water that died before it reached the shore.

definition: An ant; named for the smell of the nest — the urine-acid reek of a disturbed colony.
lost: Named the ant by sensory encounter rather than appearance — pismire is what you know the ant by when you have knelt in the grass and put your hand in the wrong place.
usage: The pismires had found the pantry; by morning there was a column running from the skirting board to the sugar jar with the purposefulness of a small army.

definition: A cavalry soldier armed with pistols; a fighter who carried the new handgun into close battle on horseback.
lost: Named a military type that existed only briefly — the pistoleer was the cavalry's answer to the arquebus, a role that gunpowder made and then rapidly unmade.
usage: The pistoleers came through the gap in the hedge before the musketeers had finished reloading.

definition: A mournful lament; a song of grief; also a formal legal complaint — the two meanings share the quality of articulated suffering.
lost: Held lamentation and litigation as the same act — a plaint is suffering that has found its voice and addressed someone with it, whether God or a magistrate.
usage: She made no plaint about the decision; she simply noted it, which was more effective.

definition: The full moon; or the period of its fullness — understood not as a calendar phase but as a condition the world enters, a state of maximum illumination before the slow diminishing begins.
lost: "Full moon" is a phase label. Plenilune names the experience of standing under it.
usage: At plenilune, the courtyard was bright enough to read by, and nobody slept.

definition: To pledge solemnly; to bind oneself by promise; a solemn engagement.
lost: Named the act of pledging as distinct from the pledge — to plight your troth was to perform a binding, not merely to feel one.
usage: He had plighted himself to her before witnesses, and he was not the kind of man to regard that as a formality.

definition: A sign of something momentous or calamitous about to happen; an omen of exceptional weight and clarity.
lost: Named the omen at its most serious — a portent is not a passing sign but a declaration, something the world is doing deliberately to get your attention.
usage: Three portents in a week was two more than he was prepared to ignore, and he began making arrangements.

definition: A hot drink of milk curdled with wine or ale, often spiced; a medicinal and comforting drink given to the sick and the cold.
lost: Named the drink that was both medicine and comfort — a posset was what you made for someone who was unwell, which meant warmth and care in a cup.
usage: She brought him a posset at midnight and sat with him while he drank it, which was the most useful thing she could do.

definition: A short motto or verse inscribed inside a ring or on a small gift; a concise text meant to be carried and reread.
lost: Named the personal inscription as a literary form — a posy was a poem reduced to its most portable size, intended to travel inside a piece of jewelry.
usage: The ring had a posy worn almost smooth: something beginning 'whilst I live' and ending with her name.

definition: A drink, especially an alcoholic one; the act of drinking; a draught taken with some deliberation.
lost: Named drinking as a considered act rather than a reflex — a potation is chosen, poured, and consumed with the awareness that one is consuming it.
usage: He required his morning potation before he was prepared to discuss anything of consequence.

definition: A thick soup or stew; the staple food of the medieval poor; whatever was available, boiled together in a pot.
lost: Named the meal that was not a recipe but a practice — pottage was made from what was there, which meant it was different every day and the same every day.
usage: There was pottage for supper as there had been for pottage for every supper that week, and would be for every supper for the week following.

definition: To talk at length and without substance; to chatter emptily; to produce the sounds of conversation without its content.
lost: Named talking that has forgotten its purpose — prate is speech that has mistaken its own activity for communication.
usage: He would prate for an hour about the wine and then order whatever was cheapest, which told you everything you needed to know.

definition: An omen or portent; to foretell; a feeling of what is about to occur.
lost: Gave foreboding a dignified name — presage is an omen that deserves to be heeded, not merely a feeling.
usage: The silence of the birds was presage enough; he turned back before the path bent out of sight.

definition: Sleight of hand; manual dexterity in the service of deception; the conjuror's art of making the impossible seem merely fast.
lost: Named magic as a craft skill rather than a mystery — prestidigitation removed the supernatural and replaced it with practice, which is both more honest and more impressive.
