usage: He had learned prestidigitation as a child and never lost the habit of making things disappear when he wanted to end a conversation.

definition: A male deer in its second year, carrying its first straight, unbranched antlers; a stag before it has become a stag.
lost: Named the adolescent deer with the care of people who organised their world around the hunt — the pricket was a legal and taxonomic category, not merely a young animal.
usage: A pricket had broken through the pale overnight; they found it at dawn, confused by its own enclosure.

definition: The first canonical hour; the prayer at dawn; the moment of earliest light acknowledged as a threshold requiring attention.
lost: Named the beginning of the day as an obligation rather than an accident — prime meant someone was awake and watching for the light, which is a different relationship to morning.
usage: He woke at prime not from piety but from habit, and lay listening to the birds make their own office before rising.

definition: The right of the firstborn to inherit the whole estate; the legal principle that makes birth order into destiny.
lost: Named the system that organised inheritance and therefore organised everything — primogeniture was not merely a rule about property but a rule about which children mattered, which shaped families and societies for centuries.
usage: The younger sons had all the ability and none of the inheritance, which primogeniture had arranged with its customary indifference to merit.

definition: The sound of wind moving through leaves and branches — specifically the quality of that sound, which is both plural and continuous, many things making one indistinct whispering.
lost: We have no common word for this. The sound exists everywhere; its name was lost and with it the recognition that the experience deserved one.
usage: The psithurism was constant enough that it had taken on the quality of a voice just below comprehension.

definition: Powerful; of great force or authority; having strength that is recognized and respected rather than merely possessed.
lost: Powerful describes capacity; puissant describes acknowledgment. A puissant lord was one whose power was known and understood by those who felt it.
usage: He had once been puissant enough that people stood when he entered; what remained now was the habit of expecting it.

definition: Physical beauty, especially of a striking or exceptional kind.
lost: Gave beauty a blunt, almost clinical weight — pulchritude is beauty stated as a fact, not admired from a distance.
usage: His pulchritude was the kind that photographs never quite caught, which meant it was entirely in the movement.

definition: Showing a lack of courage or determination; timid; faint-hearted — lacking the soul for what the situation requires.
lost: Named cowardice as a smallness of spirit rather than an absence of nerve — pusillanimous literally means small-souled, which is a different indictment than merely being afraid. It implies that the spirit itself is undersized.
usage: The decision was pusillanimous in a way that disappointed everyone who had expected better, including some who had not expected much.

definition: A kite; a buzzard; any large bird of prey associated with carrion and opportunism; also: a term of contempt for a grasping person.
lost: Named the scavenger bird with the same word used for the human equivalent — puttock understood that certain kinds of greed look the same in feathers or cloth.
usage: He circled every negotiation like a puttock, arriving when the hard work was done and the scraps were still warm.

definition: An impudent or badly behaved girl or woman; a bold, forward female — not a queen, but its older, rougher cognate.
lost: Queen and quean were once two spellings of related words; queen rose and quean fell, which tells you everything about which qualities were rewarded.
usage: She was called a quean for the same behavior that would have earned a man a reputation for spirit.

definition: Complaining in a petulant, whining manner; habitually fretful; the voice of someone who has given up expecting things to improve.
lost: Named the complaint that has stopped expecting results — querulous is not the anger of someone who wants change but the grievance of someone who has decided nothing will change and says so anyway.
usage: A querulous letter from his landlord that was the latest in a series he had stopped reading carefully.

definition: To give life to; the moment a fetus first moves in the womb; to become alive.
lost: Named the specific threshold between not-yet-living and living — quicken is the moment, not the state, and that distinction is irreplaceable.
usage: She felt it quicken on a Tuesday, and afterward the whole pregnancy was different.

definition: The essential nature of a thing; the quality that makes it what it is rather than anything else; the whatness of something.
lost: Named the philosophical question 'what is it?' as a noun — the quiddity of a thing is the answer to that question, the irreducible quality that you would lose if you removed it and still had the thing.
usage: She couldn't name the quiddity of the place, only that its absence would have made it somewhere else entirely.

definition: An inquisitive, gossipy person; one perpetually asking 'what now?' — from the Latin for that exact question.
lost: Named the gossip with a precision that 'busybody' lacks — a quidnunc is specifically addicted to news, to the next thing, to the currency of information.
usage: Every village kept its quidnunc the way it kept its blacksmith: necessary, occasionally useful, best approached with care.

definition: A discharge from a debt or obligation; a receipt that closes the account.
lost: Named the specific moment when something is fully paid and therefore done — quittance is the sound of a ledger closing.
usage: She demanded quittance in writing before she would leave the room.

definition: Daily; recurring every day; the ordinary and habitual that wears its repetition openly.
lost: Named the daily not as routine but as fact — quotidian is neither pejorative nor celebratory; it simply acknowledges that some things happen every day and that this shapes them.
usage: The quotidian demands of the farm left no space for the extraordinary, which was either a hardship or a mercy depending on the year.

definition: To be driven before the wind, as a cloud or mist; to drift in visible movement across the sky.
lost: We kept rack only as a torture device and a ruin — lost the cloud-word, which named movement that was neither directed nor aimless but simply carried.
usage: The clouds racked overhead all morning, shredding and reforming without ever quite becoming rain.

definition: Damp, foggy, and raw; the particular cold of wet air before a damp wind; weather that gets into the bones.
lost: Named a compound quality that requires several modern words — rawky is cold and wet and clinging together, a weather with personality.
usage: A rawky morning, the kind that made old men's joints predict things and young ones grateful for wool.

definition: Counsel, advice — particularly advice given by one who has seen or suffered more, whose knowledge comes not from study but from consequence. Also: a riddle; to interpret.
lost: "Advice" is offered casually. Rede implies the advisor has looked at something directly and shares what they found there.
usage: She had given her rede plainly and without softening, and he had not thanked her for it.

definition: A small temporary fortification; an outwork defending a position; anything formidable and difficult to overcome.
lost: Named the temporary made permanent by necessity — a redoubt was built fast and held desperately, fortification as improvisation.
usage: They held the redoubt for six days before the order came to abandon it, which felt like a betrayal.

definition: Smoke, steam, or vapor rising; warm mist exuding from the earth or water; the visible breath of heated things.
lost: We kept reek only as stench — lost the broader sense of any visible vapor or steam rising, which named fog as exhalation and smoke as weather.
usage: A reek rose from the wet cobbles as the afternoon sun finally reached them, the street breathing out its stored rain.

definition: Shining brightly; radiant; casting a warm and brilliant light that seems to pour from within.
lost: Cousin to fulgent and effulgent — the family of Latin fulgere words that named different qualities of shine. Refulgent is brightness that reflects and returns, light bounced back into the world.
usage: A refulgent morning after a week of rain that made everything look as if it had been recently invented.

definition: To be fretfully discontented; to chafe against one's circumstances without the energy to change them.
lost: Named a specific emotional mode between resignation and complaint — repine is the unhappiness of someone who has given up fighting but not given up minding.
usage: She did not complain exactly; she repined, which was worse, because it had no object you could address.

definition: Payment in kind; the return of a deed, whether good or ill, in equivalent measure.
lost: Held both revenge and gratitude as the same structural act — requital is balance restored, not emotion expressed.
usage: He waited years for requital; when it came, it was more quiet than he had imagined.

definition: Shining brilliantly; dazzlingly colourful and impressive; radiating light and splendour.
lost: Named the dazzle that demands attention — resplendent things are not merely bright but commanding, light deployed as authority.
usage: The resplendent absurdity of the ceremonial uniform made it impossible to take the wearer seriously, which was perhaps the point.

definition: A person who returns from a long absence as if from the dead; a ghost; one who comes back changed enough that they seem to belong to neither world properly.
lost: Named the return that disturbs — a revenant is not merely someone who went away and came back, but someone whose return is uncanny, who carries the elsewhere in them visibly.
usage: He came back from the war a revenant — present in the house but not quite in the room, looking at things from a distance that no rearrangement of furniture could close.

definition: Watery discharge from the eyes, nose, or mouth; the cold made visible; mucus as evidence of what the body is enduring.
lost: Named the body's weeping in a register that is neither metaphorical nor purely clinical — rheum is what the body produces when it is sad or sick, and the word does not distinguish.
usage: His eyes ran with rheum in the cold air, which gave him the permanent appearance of having just heard very bad news.

definition: Steam, smoke, or vapor; a thick, warm fog; the visible breath of the earth.
lost: Named fog by what it resembles — roke is fog understood as exhalation, as if the ground were breathing.
usage: A roke rose from the marshes as the temperature dropped, turning the reeds to grey shapes and then to nothing.

definition: An ordinary riding horse; not a destrier, not a palfrey — the horse of merchants, pilgrims, and people who need to get somewhere.
lost: Named the horse of the middle register, which is most horses and most people — the rouncey was the reliable, unremarkable animal that actually moved the medieval world.
usage: The monk rode a rouncey that had made the pilgrimage four times already and knew the road better than its rider.

definition: To feel bitter regret; to wish something undone; to experience the specific grief of what cannot be reversed.
lost: We kept rue as a herb and in rueful but lost the verb at its full weight — rue is regret that understands its own futility and continues anyway.
usage: He rued the letter from the moment he posted it, which was too late by a quarter of a mile.

definition: A letter of the early Germanic alphabet; a mystery, a secret, or a whispered word; any inscription believed to carry magical force.
lost: Named the letter and the secret in one word — runes were not merely characters but encoded knowledge, the written form of things that could not be spoken openly.
usage: He left three runes carved in the lintel that no one could interpret, which had been the intention.

definition: A candle made from a rush dipped in tallow; the cheapest light available; the illumination of the poor.
lost: Named light at its most precarious — a rushlight gave barely enough to work by and lasted less than an hour, which shaped how evenings were spent in most households in England for centuries.
usage: She worked by rushlight until it guttered, and then she stopped, because there was no point making more waste.

definition: Compassion; pity for another's suffering; a tender sorrow that moves toward rather than away from pain.
lost: We kept ruthless but abandoned ruth — which means we have a word for the absence of compassion but not for the thing itself. The asymmetry is revealing.
usage: She felt ruth for the man, which surprised her, given everything she knew about him.

definition: Black, especially in heraldry; a deep, formal black that implies mourning or gravity.
lost: Gave black a register — sable black is serious, heraldic, carried; it is not merely the absence of color.
usage: He dressed in sable from collar to heel, and no one asked why.

definition: A sudden charge from a besieged position; the defenders pouring out to strike the besiegers before retreating back inside.
lost: Named the desperate offensive of those who are losing — a sally is aggression born of desperation, attack as the only form of defence left.
usage: They made three sallies in the first week; by the fourth they lacked the men for it.

definition: A gesture or utterance of greeting; the formal acknowledgment of another person's presence; hello understood as a ceremony.
lost: Named greeting as a performed act — a salutation is not casual acknowledgment but a formal recognition, the meeting of two people conducted according to rules that say the meeting matters.
usage: He gave the salutation of the order, which the stranger returned correctly, which changed the nature of the conversation entirely.

definition: Having the power to heal; tending to cure; pertaining to the restorative properties of a substance or place.
lost: Healing is a verb; sanative is a quality — it describes what a thing inherently is, not what it does on a given occasion.
usage: The air of the coast was considered sanative, which was why the wealthy went there and the poor stayed behind.

definition: Blood-red in color; also: optimistic, confident, of a hopeful temperament — from the belief that blood was the humor of cheerfulness.
lost: Held the color and the character in the same word because they were once believed to share a cause — the loss of that connection drained both meanings.
usage: He was sanguine about the delay in a way that suggested he knew something the rest of them didn't.

definition: To walk in a slow, idle manner without purpose or urgency; to move through space as if space were not the point. Thoreau derived it from 'sainte terre' — holy land — the pilgrim who walks toward something sacred.
lost: Whether Thoreau's etymology is correct or not, it changed what the word meant — a saunterer became, in his reading, someone walking toward rather than without destination, which is either a beautiful redemption of aimlessness or an imposition of purpose where none existed.
usage: He sauntered across the plaza in a way that suggested he had either nowhere to be or somewhere very good to be, and she couldn't tell which.

definition: A small portion or specimen; a sample used to judge the whole; also: a thin timber of specified dimensions.
lost: Named the representative fragment — a scantling is not the thing itself but sufficient to know the thing by, which is a precise and useful epistemological concept.
usage: A scantling of his conversation was enough; she had heard everything she needed to hear.

definition: A reckless, mischievous person who has escaped the grace of God; a rascal beyond redemption's reach.
lost: Named the charming irredeemable — a scapegrace is not a villain but someone who has simply outrun every available blessing.
usage: He was the family scapegrace, beloved and unreliable in equal measure, and everyone had long since stopped expecting otherwise.

definition: A spark; the smallest possible trace or fragment of something.
lost: Named the unit of light that makes fire conceivable — a scintilla is light at the threshold of existence.
usage: There was not a scintilla of warmth in his reply, and everyone present understood.

definition: One who pretends to knowledge they do not possess; a superficial scholar; someone who mistakes familiarity for understanding.
lost: Named the specific failure of half-learning — a sciolist has read enough to sound convincing and not enough to know they shouldn't.
usage: The lecture circuit was full of sciolists that year, each one fluent in the vocabulary of fields they'd visited once.

definition: To see visions in a reflective or dark surface — water, glass, a mirror turned face-down; to seek knowledge through a medium that offers images without explanation.
lost: "Prophesy" implies certainty about the future. Scry names the method — looking into something dark until it shows you something true, with no guarantee of interpretation.
usage: She could scry but had long since stopped; the images were reliable but the meanings were not.

definition: To move rapidly before the wind; of clouds or rain: to drive in sheets driven by gale; also: the low ragged cloud driven thus.
lost: Named the wind's momentum transferred to weather — scud is rain and cloud not falling but being sent, propelled by something invisible and purposeful.
usage: Scud was racing across the headland when they set out, which they chose to interpret as promising rather than otherwise.

definition: A small room off the kitchen for washing dishes and dirty work; the part of the house where the kitchen's least pleasant tasks were performed.
lost: Named the hierarchy of domestic space — the scullery was below the kitchen as the kitchen was below the dining room, a geography of labour and status expressed in architecture.
usage: The scullery maid worked from five until nine with her hands in cold water, which was considered neither noteworthy nor cruel.

definition: Dry flakes of skin; dandruff; any dry, scaling matter that peels from a surface; the skin's refusal to stay on.
lost: Named bodily decay at its most visible and least dramatic — scurf is the body shedding what it no longer needs, which it does constantly and without asking.
usage: The collar of his coat was dusted with scurf, which he either hadn't noticed or had decided not to.

definition: Grossly offensive language; crude insult; low, foul verbal abuse that descends below the register of wit into something more basic.
lost: Named insult at its least refined — scurrility is the verbal equivalent of throwing mud, abuse that has given up on being clever and just wants to soil.
usage: The pamphlet descended into scurrility by the third page, which lost it what little authority the first two had established.

definition: Everlasting, eternal — but with emphasis on temporal continuity rather than spatial extension; what has always been and will continue being, regardless of what else changes.
lost: "Eternal" is spatial — it goes on. Sempiternal emphasizes the temporal — it precedes you and will outlast you.
usage: The sempiternal indifference of the mountain was, in its own way, a comfort.

definition: The process of growing old; the biological deterioration that accompanies age; ageing understood as an active process rather than a passive accumulation of years.
lost: Named ageing as something the body does — senescence is not the mere passage of time but the cellular and physiological change that time produces, which makes it more honest and more precise than simply 'growing old.'
usage: The senescence of the building matched his own so exactly that he had stopped distinguishing between the two.

definition: A period of seven nights; a week — but measured by darkness rather than days. Time as endurance rather than administration.
lost: "Week" is administrative. Sennight implies the weight of seven sleeps endured.
usage: He had not eaten in a sennight, and the forest showed no signs of ending.

definition: Of or suggestive of a tomb; hollow and deep in sound; gloomy with the specific gravity of burial.
lost: Named a quality of atmosphere that takes the tomb as its reference point — sepulchral darkness is not merely dim but specifically deathly, enclosed, resonant with what it contains.
usage: His voice had a sepulchral quality that was either naturally deep or carefully cultivated; the result was the same.

definition: Fine rain falling from a clear sky; rain that falls in the evening from a sky with no visible cloud — the impossible precipitation.
lost: Named rain's most uncanny form — serein falls without a source, drizzles from nothing, makes you wet from a sky that offers no explanation. The French word for calm evening air somehow attached itself to this paradox.
usage: They sat outside in a serein so light it was more felt than seen, a dampness arriving from the cloudless sky above them.

definition: A slaughterhouse; a place of butchery; a scene of blood and disorder — before it became merely a synonym for mess.
lost: The word survived its meaning's collapse — shambles now means any disorder, but it once meant the specific violent disorder of a place designed for killing.
usage: The street that ran behind the shambles smelled of what it was adjacent to, and the property values showed it.

definition: To hear a confession and grant absolution; to impose penance; to cleanse a soul through the formal act of a priest.
lost: Named absolution as a specific priestly technology — to shrive someone was to perform a sacramental act, not merely to forgive them, which is a very different thing.
usage: He had not been shriven in years and carried the weight of that accumulation in a way that showed in his posture.

definition: A hissing sound; the production of hissing consonants; the sound of air through narrow spaces.
lost: Gave the hiss a name of its own — sibilation is a sonic object, not just a description.
usage: The sibilation of the gas jet was the only sound in the corridor.

definition: Oracular, mysteriously prophetic; of or resembling a sibyl.
lost: Encoded the specific register of feminine prophecy — not wild, but precise and deeply inconvenient.
usage: Her answer was sibylline enough that everyone in the room found a different warning in it.

definition: A position that requires little or no work but provides income or honour; an office without cure of souls — without pastoral responsibility.
lost: Named the reward without the obligation — a sinecure is a position stripped of its actual function while retaining its benefits, which the church invented and the state perfected.
usage: The post had become a sinecure over the decades, the actual work distributed quietly to others while the title and salary remained.

definition: A hot, oppressive wind blowing from North Africa across the Mediterranean; a wind that carries heat and dust and a specific kind of exhaustion.
lost: Named the wind that travels — the sirocco crosses the sea and arrives on southern European shores still carrying the Sahara in it, hot and dry and laden with fine red dust that settles on everything and makes people irrational.
usage: A sirocco had been blowing for four days and everyone's nerves were raw in the specific way that wind produces.

definition: Since that time; from that point forward; afterward — a word that places the speaker after an event and measuring distance from it.
lost: Since collapsed the temporal and causal into one word; sithence was purely temporal, purely about time elapsed, which gave it a different weight.
usage: He had not returned to the village sithence, nor sent word, nor been heard from by anyone who would say.

definition: The shrill, piercing sound of bagpipes; any high, sustained, penetrating cry; sound at the edge of endurance.
lost: Named a specific pitch of sound — skirl is not just loud but specifically piercing, the sound that gets through walls and wind and refuses to be muffled.
usage: The pipes skirled from the head of the glen before they could see the procession, and the sound made the hair rise.

definition: A minor fight between small detachments; an engagement that is not quite a battle; conflict at the edges.
lost: Named the fight that doesn't count in the histories but kills you just as thoroughly — a skirmish is war experienced by the people who never appear in the accounts.
usage: There had been skirmishes along the border all summer; nobody declared war, which meant nobody had to stop.

definition: Rain so fine it barely falls; a drizzle that is closer to mist than to rain, felt on the face before it is seen.
lost: Named the threshold between weather and atmosphere — smirr is what happens when the air is too wet to be called dry but too thin to be called rain.
usage: Not rain exactly — more a smirr that made the cobblestones gleam without quite wetting them.

definition: Bitterly cold; sharp and piercing, of wind or weather; keen in a way that cuts rather than merely chills.
lost: Cold tells you about temperature; snell tells you about intent — a snell wind is cold that seems to mean it.
usage: A snell easterly had been blowing since morning, and by afternoon it had emptied the market square.

definition: A temporary stay; a brief residence in a place that is not home; to dwell for a time without putting down roots.
lost: Named the stay that knows it is temporary — a sojourn is defined by its own transience, which gives it a quality that residence lacks. You know when you arrive at a sojourn that you will leave it.
usage: His sojourn in the city lasted eleven years, which by any reasonable measure had ceased to be temporary, but he continued to treat it as such.

definition: One who wanders alone; specifically, one who prefers the solitary road and seeks it out rather than merely tolerating it.
lost: "Loner" carries pathology. Solivagant carries intention.
usage: A confirmed solivagant, she kept no fixed address and required none.

definition: Truth; reality as it actually is; what is genuinely the case, as opposed to what is said or believed.
lost: We kept sooth only in soothsayer — the truth-teller, the person who has access to sooth — which means we kept the specialist but lost the thing they specialise in.
usage: In sooth he had known for months; what he lacked was not knowledge but a way to act on it.

definition: Truthful; faithful to the truth; firmly anchored in what is real — not merely honest by policy but by nature.
lost: Named truth as a quality you hold rather than a rule you follow — soothfast people are truthful because they cannot be otherwise, which is different from choosing not to lie.
usage: He was known as soothfast, which meant that what he said could be relied upon, and also that he said things other people would have thought better of.

definition: One who foretells the future; a prophet or diviner — literally one who says sooth, who speaks truth about what has not yet happened.
lost: Named the prophet by what they do rather than what they are — a soothsayer says the sooth, tells the truth, which implies that prophecy is not invention but revelation, not imagination but knowledge.
usage: The soothsayer in the market charged less than the physician and was, on balance, about as useful.

definition: A reddish-brown colour; a horse or deer of that colour; a specific warmth of brown that has red in it.
lost: Named a colour by the creature most often seen in it — sorel is horse-brown, the warm red-brown of well-kept coats and autumn bracken.
usage: A sorel mare of perhaps fifteen hands, with a white stripe and the look of a horse who had been somewhere and remembered it.

definition: Divination by the casting or drawing of lots; by extension, any practice that uses chance as a vehicle for revelation — the faith that randomness, properly attended to, is not random.
lost: "Divination" is the category. Sortilege names the specific belief — that chance does not obscure the truth but reveals it.
usage: She distrusted sortilege on principle, though the lots had never once been wrong.

definition: A soft moaning or sighing sound — specifically the noise the wind makes moving through things: trees, wire, the gaps in old structures. Not howling, not murmuring, but the exact register between them.
lost: "Howl" is too strong; "murmur" is too gentle. Sough names the specific register — wind in things, at the edge of meaning.
usage: The sough of the rigging was company of a kind, if not conversation.

definition: The measured depth of water beneath a ship; the act of measuring that depth with a lead line; to be in soundings is to be in water shallow enough to reach the bottom — a known and manageable world. To lose soundings is to enter the deep ocean.
lost: Named the boundary between the known and the unknown — in soundings, you could anchor, you could navigate by depth, you had a relationship to the bottom. Out of soundings, you were in the open ocean, depth unmeasurable, the bottom irrelevant and unreachable. The transition between the two was the most significant crossing a sailor made.
usage: They took soundings every hour as they closed the coast, the leadsman calling the depths from the chains in a voice that carried aft to the helmsman.

definition: Irritable and bad-tempered from melancholy; sharp-tongued as a symptom of inner depletion.
lost: Named the specific personality produced by too much black bile — splenetic is not merely grumpy but precisely unwell in a way that makes you cruel.
usage: He grew splenetic by late afternoon each day, which the staff had learned to interpret as hunger.

definition: A starling; the bird of collective flight and stolen songs; named before it was studied.
lost: Starling replaced starnel and lost half the word's sound — starnel has the right strangeness for a bird that is iridescent in one light and black in another.
usage: A cloud of starnel lifted from the elms at dusk and moved in one body across the valley in a shape that kept changing its mind.

definition: A farmstead; a group of farm buildings; the whole domestic establishment of a working farm considered as a unit.
lost: Named the farm as a complete domestic world — a steading was not just fields but house, byre, barn, yard, and all the relationships between them.
usage: The steading had been in the family for six generations, which was visible in the different ages of the buildings and the way they faced each other.

definition: Movement of a vessel backwards through the water; way made in the wrong direction; the ship going where her stern points.
lost: Named the wrong direction as its own kind of progress — a ship making sternway is moving, just not where she is aimed, and the distinction matters enormously when there is something behind you. Everything that steers a ship is designed for forward motion; sternway makes her unpredictable.
usage: She was making sternway before they had the anchor clear, which was when he understood how much water there was behind them.

definition: To wander aimlessly, without destination or the need of one — specifically implying that the aimlessness is preferred, not lamented; a chosen vacancy of purpose.
lost: "Wander" suggests purposelessness. Stravage suggests the purposelessness is the point.
usage: He had stravaged the moors since boyhood and saw no reason to develop a destination.

definition: Of or belonging to the Styx; utterly, inescapably dark; of the underworld.
lost: Gave darkness a proper address — not just the absence of light but a specific geography of hopelessness.
usage: The stygian water of the flooded mine swallowed their torchlight completely.

definition: A pack horse or mule; a beast whose purpose is to carry what humans cannot; the animal of all labor and no glory.
lost: Named the working animal by its function with a dignity the modern 'pack horse' refuses — a sumpter carried the world, and the word acknowledged the weight.
usage: The sumpters were loaded before dawn, each one bearing its allocated weight with the resignation of long practice.

definition: A pledge or guarantee; one who answers for another's obligations; the person who stakes themselves on someone else's word.
lost: Named the guarantor as a distinct and serious role — a surety puts their own standing at risk on behalf of another person's promise.
usage: He offered himself as surety for the debt, which was either very confident or very foolish.

definition: A continuous whispering or murmuring sound; the act of murmuring.
lost: Sister to susurrus but a process, not a state — susurration is the ongoing act, the duration of the sound.
usage: A susurration ran through the congregation before the doors opened.

definition: A whisper, murmur, or continuous soft sound — the quality of voices or water or machinery at the edge of hearing; sound that exists at the threshold between information and atmosphere.
lost: "Whisper" requires a speaker. Susurrus names the quality of the sound itself, independent of its origin.
usage: There was a susurrus from the walls that she had learned not to investigate.

definition: A sovereign who exercises authority over a vassal state; the overlord in a relationship of hierarchical sovereignty; the lord above the lord.
lost: Named the layer of authority above authority — a suzerain is not merely a king but the king that other kings answer to, which is a specific and complicated relationship.
usage: He acknowledged no suzerain, which was either admirable independence or a declaration of war depending on whose perspective you took.

definition: Dark, dusky, swarthy; the specific darkness of a complexion or landscape with warmth in the shadow.
lost: Dark and swarthy both derive from swart but dropped the original — swarthy means dark with a pejorative connotation the word never originally carried.
usage: A swart evening had settled over the valley, the kind of darkness that is warm rather than cold and that smells of turned earth.

definition: A dream or vision — particularly one that arrives with weight, that carries the quality of communication, as if something were attempting to reach you through sleep.
lost: "Dream" is neutral. Sweven implies the dream was sent rather than merely happening.
usage: The sweven returned three nights running, always ending at the same locked door.

definition: Originally the bird before it was the adjective; the swift was named for its quality before the quality had a separate word.
lost: The bird gave the language its word for speed — swift was a name before it was a description, and the bird is still the best argument for what the word means.
usage: The swifts arrived on the same date they always did, screaming low over the rooftops in a way that meant summer had decided.

definition: To toil; to labour with the body; to work until the work has taken something from you that rest does not fully restore.
lost: Work is neutral; swink is not. To swink is to spend yourself in labour, which implies a cost the word does not minimize or celebrate.
usage: They had swinked since before light and the field was half-done, which was either encouraging or not depending on the weather.

definition: To faint; a sudden loss of consciousness; the body's surrender to an overload it cannot process upright.
lost: Named the body's retreat with more dignity than 'faint' allows — a swoon implies you were feeling something worth losing consciousness over.
usage: She did not swoon at the news; she went very still, which was more alarming.

definition: Weariness of life; the exhaustion of existence itself; not depression exactly but the fatigue of continuing, the specific tiredness of someone who has been alive long enough.
lost: Named the weariness that is not illness but experience — taedium vitae is what happens when the world has shown you enough of itself that novelty no longer arrives, and what remains is the effort of continuing to pay attention.
usage: He was not unhappy exactly; it was more a taedium vitae, a sense that the menu was familiar and he had lost his appetite.

definition: Rendered animal fat used for candles and lubrication; the substance that made artificial light possible for most of history.
lost: Named the material that lit the world before wax became affordable — tallow candles smoked and smelled and dripped, but they were the difference between darkness and the ability to see after sunset.
usage: The tallow candles left a smell that clung to everything, which is how you knew which houses could not afford wax.

definition: Grief, suffering, injury; harm done to the spirit as well as the body; affliction that is both wound and sorrow.
lost: We kept teen only as an age bracket, which is its least interesting application — in Old and Middle English it named the quality of suffering with a precision that grief and pain separately lack.
usage: The news brought him teen he had not expected; he had thought himself past the age of being surprised by loss.

definition: Profound, sacred shadow; the darkness of Holy Week; shadows as a liturgical event.
lost: Named darkness as something attended and witnessed rather than merely suffered.
usage: She sat in the tenebrae of the nave long after the candles had been extinguished one by one.

definition: The painting technique of extreme contrast between light and dark; Caravaggio's method of placing intense illumination against near-total darkness.
lost: Named a way of seeing rather than a way of painting — tenebrism understood that the most revealing light is the light that shows you what darkness contains, which is an epistemology as much as an aesthetic.
usage: The scene had a natural tenebrism to it — one lamp, the rest of the room in complete dark, the faces lit from below.

definition: Dark, shadowed, obscure — specifically a darkness that has qualities, texture, weight; a darkness that feels inhabited rather than merely unlit.
lost: "Dark" is a condition. Tenebrous implies the darkness is doing something.
usage: The passage grew tenebrous long before the torches had any reason to fail.

definition: To make evasive or contradictory statements; to change sides repeatedly; to be deliberately unclear in order to avoid commitment.
lost: Named evasion as a systematic practice rather than a single instance — tergiversation is a policy, not a lapse, which requires its own word.
usage: He tergiversated through the entire interview and emerged having said nothing that could be quoted against him, which was the point.

definition: Magic performed with the aid of beneficent spirits; the attempt to compel divine action through ritual; the higher magic that works with rather than against the sacred.
lost: Named the magic that considered itself legitimate — theurgy was the practitioner's distinction between their own work and mere sorcery, a moral taxonomy of the supernatural.
usage: He called it theurgy rather than magic, which was a distinction he considered important and his neighbours did not.

definition: Muscular strength; the visible quality of bodily power; sinew and vigor as a physical presence.
lost: Gave strength a texture — thew is not an abstract capacity but something you can see in the shoulders of someone who has it.
usage: He had the thew of someone who had carried things for a living, and it showed in the way he stood.

definition: To endure; to bear suffering patiently; to put up with without flinching and without complaint.
lost: Endure implies that the suffering is finite — you will get through it. Thole makes no such promise. To thole is to bear without expectation of an end, which is a different and heavier act.
usage: She had tholed three winters of it without saying anything that would have been useful to say, and by the fourth she had stopped counting.

definition: Bondage; a person held in bondage; captivity that is complete and not merely temporary.
lost: Named slavery with the weight of capture — a thrall is not merely unfree but held, which is a different and heavier thing.
usage: In thrall to an opinion he could not quite defend, he defended it anyway.

definition: A low, continuous, rhythmic vibrating sound; to produce such a sound; the hum of something under tension.
lost: Named sound as a physical property of objects rather than an event — a thrum is what wires, bowstrings, and taut membranes do when they are alive with tension.
usage: The rigging thrummed in the wind at a pitch that changed with the weather and that experienced sailors could read.

definition: A sudden, heavy shower accompanying thunder; rain that arrives as a mass rather than a stream.
lost: Named the specific violence of a storm downpour — a thunder-plump is not heavy rain but rain delivered all at once, as if emptied rather than falling.
usage: A thunder-plump at noon sent everyone scrambling for the market hall, and the gutters ran white within seconds.

definition: The male of any hawk, especially the peregrine; smaller than the female by roughly a third, hence the name — tierce, a third.
lost: Named the male falcon by his size relative to the female — falconers named the smaller bird the third, because the female was the standard and the male the deviation, which is a useful inversion.
usage: He flew the tiercel for speed; the female he kept for the heronry, where size and power mattered more than quickness.

definition: The sound of bells ringing; the continuous, overlapping music of struck metal.
lost: Named a sonic texture that 'ringing' flattens — tintinnabulation is bells as an environment, not an event.
usage: The tintinnabulation from the cathedral filled the square every quarter-hour until it became part of how silence felt.

definition: A fox; the hunting name for the animal that organized an entire social ritual around its pursuit.
lost: Named the fox as the quarry — tod is the fox seen from horseback, as the object of a ceremony, which is a very specific angle of vision.
usage: A tod had been working the lower coverts for weeks before they laid a trail and found nothing.

definition: Sluggish, dormant, numbed to sensation; the state of a creature in winter suspension.
lost: Named the specific quality of hibernation-in-humans — torpid is not tired, it is the system slowing to minimum.
usage: He spent the winter months torpid, emerging only when the cold required it.

definition: A medieval tournament; a series of jousting and martial contests organised as spectacle, competition, and military training.
lost: Named the war game that was also entertainment — the tourney was violence made safe enough to be watched by ladies, which required specific rules about what could and couldn't be done to a dismounted knight.
usage: The tourney had drawn knights from four counties, and the prize was enough to make the journey worthwhile even for those who expected to lose.

definition: From beyond the mountains; a stranger or foreigner; also: a cold north wind from over the Alps.
lost: Named the stranger by their origin rather than their strangeness — a tramontane is defined by what they crossed, not what they lack.
usage: They regarded him as tramontane: arriving from somewhere hard, knowing things the valley didn't.

definition: Hard, painful labor; anguished effort; the specific suffering of work that costs more than it returns.
lost: We kept travel from the same root but lost travail — both words were once the same, because all travel was hard labor. Their separation is a measure of how comfortable movement became.
usage: The travail of the crossing had left them thin and quarrelsome, and landfall only partly helped.

definition: A thick slice of bread used as a plate; later a wooden plate; a flat surface on which food was served and eaten.
lost: Named the plate that was also food — the trencher was eaten or given to the poor after the meal, which meant nothing was wasted and the plate was always fresh.
usage: The trenchers at the lower tables were stale bread; at the high table they were wood, which was considered an improvement.

definition: Pledged faith, sworn loyalty — the word used when loyalty is not merely held but formally given, bound by the act of speaking it. To plight one's troth is to transfer something irretrievable.
lost: "Loyalty" is a virtue you hold. Troth is a commitment you make — the binding is in the speaking.
usage: She had given her troth in the old form, and considered herself held by it regardless of what had followed.

definition: To believe, suppose, or trust in a thing — with a quality of quiet, considered conviction. Not a guess but a held conclusion.
lost: "Think" is provisional. Trow implies the belief has been weighed and kept.
usage: "He'll not be coming back, I trow," said the innkeeper, and refilled the glass.

definition: A sharp vibrating sound as of a plucked string; a nasal quality in a voice; the sound of tension suddenly released.
lost: Named the sound of a string's whole life compressed into one moment — a twang is the bow after the arrow, the instrument after the pluck, resonance as aftermath.
usage: The twang of the bowstring was the last sound he heard before the return shot, which he did not hear at all.

definition: A full year; twelve months understood as a counted unit rather than a calendar abstraction.
lost: Year names the cycle; twelvemonth counts it — the word holds the year as a span of months you lived through, not a unit of celestial mechanics.
usage: They agreed to meet again in a twelvemonth, which at the time seemed like a manageable distance.

definition: To reproach or taunt with a past fault; to remind someone of something they would rather forget, with the intent of shaming.
lost: We kept twit only as 'fool' — lost the verb that named a specific verbal act, the calculated reminder of failure designed to wound rather than instruct.
usage: He twitted her with the letter for years, producing it whenever he needed to end an argument, until it had lost all effect through overuse.

definition: A sustained wail or howl; a rising, modulated cry that carries intensity itself rather than any specific emotion — the vocal form of extremity, equally available to grief and ecstasy.
lost: "Wailing" implies grief. Ululation names the sound — the intensity that precedes and outlasts whatever specific feeling produced it.
usage: The ululation rose from somewhere in the lower city and did not stop until dawn.

definition: The fully shadowed region of an eclipse where no light reaches; the darkest part of any shadow; darkness as a precise geometric fact.
lost: Named darkness with scientific exactness — the umbra is not any shadow but the shadow where the source is completely blocked, total darkness as a calculable phenomenon rather than an atmospheric condition.
usage: They had driven three hundred miles to stand in the umbra for four minutes, which was long enough.

definition: Of or relating to shadow; dwelling in shadow; the darkest part of an eclipse.
lost: Gave shade a technical precision — the umbra is the zone where no light survives at all.
usage: An umbral calm settled over the garden as the eclipse reached its full depth.

definition: Staying in the shade; reclusive; averse to open roads and exposure.
lost: Named the counter-wanderer — the person who stays is also a type, and umbratic gave them precision.
usage: His umbratic disposition made him excellent at correspondence and useless on any kind of expedition.

definition: The right to enjoy the use and benefits of another's property without damaging or diminishing it; stewardship without ownership.
lost: Named the middle ground between ownership and tenancy — usufruct is the right to use without the right to dispose, which encodes a specific relationship between people and property that modern law has mostly replaced with simpler categories.
usage: She had usufruct of the house during her lifetime, after which it passed to the trust — a distinction that mattered enormously and was invisible to everyone who visited.

definition: The cry of a newborn; the first sound made at the opening of a life.
lost: Named the cry that begins everything — the only sound with no precedent in the life that produces it.
usage: A vagitus from the back room told him the ordeal was over.

definition: A farewell; the words spoken at a departure; the formal act of saying goodbye in a way that acknowledges what is being left.
lost: Goodbye is a single word; valediction is a speech act — the formal ceremony of departure, the thing you say when leaving requires more than a word.
usage: His valediction ran to twenty minutes and nobody left early, which was the best thing that could be said about it.

definition: Armour for the forearm; the plate protecting the lower arm from wrist to elbow.
lost: Named the specific protection of the sword arm — a vambrace was the difference between a blocked blow and a severed limb, and its name knew exactly what it was defending.
usage: The vambrace had taken a cut that would have ended the fight otherwise; he noted it afterward with something like gratitude.

definition: A nervous condition; depression, hysteria, or faintness attributed to noxious exhalations rising from the stomach; the malady of sensitive constitutions.
lost: Named a category of distress that we have since divided into anxiety, depression, and hypochondria — the vapours held them together as one condition with one cause.
usage: She took to her room with the vapours every time the question of the estate was raised, which was taken as commentary rather than illness.

definition: To prophesy; to speak as an oracle; to foretell with authority.
lost: Named the act of prophecy as a specific verbal performance, not just a lucky guess.
usage: The old man would vaticinate from the steps each morning, and the fishermen had long since stopped laughing.

definition: Food and provisions; sustenance — spelled as if Latin but pronounced as if the word knows better.
lost: The spelling-pronunciation gap is the word's most interesting feature — victuals looks learned but sounds vernacular, which is the trace of a pronunciation shift the spelling never followed. The word was always more honest in the mouth than on the page.
usage: They had victuals enough for a week if they were careful, and the kind of people who are careful about victuals generally are.

definition: A serf; a person bound to the land and to the service of a lord; one whose labor is owned rather than chosen.
lost: Named the bound person with more precision than 'serf' — a villein had specific legal status, specific obligations, specific rights within that subjection. The word carried the whole system.
usage: He was born a villein and his children would be born villein; this was not cruelty but law, which made it worse.

definition: Streaks of precipitation that evaporate before reaching the ground; rain that falls from cloud but never arrives; a visible promise the sky fails to keep.
lost: Named the failed rain — virga is precipitation in process, rain that begins to fall and then is absorbed by dry air before it completes the journey. From below it looks like the cloud is crying without tears reaching anything.
usage: Virga hung from the clouds all afternoon, trailing grey streaks over the desert that made the air smell of rain without providing any.

definition: Green; fresh; alive with the particular green of growing things.
lost: Gave green a specific register — virid is not any green but the green of things that are growing because they have no choice.
usage: The hillside was virid in a way that felt aggressive after months of grey.

definition: Bitter and sustained verbal abuse; prolonged fault-finding delivered with maximum unpleasantness; the full deployment of language as a weapon.
lost: Named abuse as a practice rather than an outburst — vituperation is not a moment of anger but a sustained campaign of hostile speech, which requires its own word.
usage: The letter was three pages of vituperation so precise that it seemed to have been drafted over several days, which made it considerably worse to receive.

definition: A female fox; also an ill-tempered or quarrelsome woman — the fox's character attributed to certain women as an insult that contained a grudging recognition of sharpness.
lost: One of the few animal words that transferred its gendered sense into human usage — a vixen is specifically female, specifically fox, specifically attributed with a kind of cunning that the word doesn't quite condemn.
usage: The vixen denned under the shed each year and was left to it, being considered more useful than the alternative.

definition: Wooden panelling lining the lower walls of a room; the specific finish that marked a room as finished and warm.
lost: Named the interior finish that changed how a room felt — wainscot turned a plastered wall into a panelled one, which was warmer, quieter, and unmistakably a different economic register.
usage: The wainscot was dark oak and ran to shoulder height, above which the walls were painted a colour that had once been fashionable.

definition: Pale, faint, depleted of color and vitality; the appearance of something that has given what it had. Not merely pale but specifically the pallor of depletion.
lost: "Pale" describes appearance. Wan describes depletion — the color has gone because something else went with it.
usage: He returned wan and said almost nothing for the first week.

definition: A strong innate desire to rove or travel; the compulsion to keep moving borrowed from German into English in the early twentieth century.
lost: The German Wanderlust is more precise than the English use allows — it is specifically the lust, the desire, not the act of wandering but the wanting-to, which is a different and more restless condition.
usage: The wanderlust that had served her well at twenty had become, by forty, something she had to negotiate with her mortgage rather than indulge.

definition: Despair; the complete failure of hope; hopelessness not as absence but as its own presence — hope that has gone wan.
lost: Named despair by what it does to hope rather than what hope's absence feels like — wanhope is hope surviving in a damaged form, which is worse than its simple absence.
usage: He had not arrived at wanhope suddenly; it had come on like winter, degree by degree, until he realised the light was gone.

definition: Ill luck; misfortune; the plague — used in imprecations: 'with a wanion' meaning to your sorrow, with ill consequences.
lost: Named bad luck as a force that could be wished upon someone — to send someone off with a wanion was to curse them with a politeness that made the curse worse.
usage: Take yourself off, with a wanion to you, and don't come back while any of us remember your face.

definition: A toast meaning 'be in good health'; a spiced ale drunk at festivities; also: to go house to house singing and drinking at Christmas.
lost: Named the ritual of communal warmth-seeking in winter — wassailing was not merely drinking but a formal procession through the cold, singing for hospitality and offering it in return.
usage: The wassailers arrived at eleven with a bucket of mulled cider and the traditional expectation of not being turned away.

definition: A traveller, especially one who travels on foot; one for whom the road is both means and purpose.
lost: Named the traveller by their relationship to the way — a wayfarer is defined by the road they are on, not by where they came from or where they are going.
usage: A wayfarer arrived at the inn near dark, ate without speaking, and was gone before the household was up.

definition: Traveling, especially on foot; the state of being one who travels roads. Used as a noun, it describes a condition of life rather than a single act of transit.
lost: "Traveling" is logistical. Wayfaring is an identity — a way of being in the world.
usage: The wayfaring had aged him less than the years spent standing still.

definition: Wellbeing; prosperity; the common good understood as a thing held collectively.
lost: Named collective prosperity as something with defenders — the common weal was a shared possession, not a policy outcome.
usage: He had served the weal his whole career and was surprised to find it had not returned the favour.

definition: A garment, especially a widow's mourning dress; the outfit worn to signal a specific grief to the world — before 'weeds' became merely an archaic word for clothing generally.
lost: We have widow's weeds in the phrase but lost the singular — a weed was a single garment of grief, the specific cloth of loss that the body wore so the world would know what it carried.
usage: She had worn the weed for a year as custom required, and then put it away, which people noticed and drew their own conclusions from.

definition: To raise the anchor from the seabed in preparation for departure; to leave a mooring. Not to throw the anchor overboard but to lift it back up — the phrase is perpetually misunderstood.
lost: Named the act of departure by its preparation — weighing anchor is not the sailing but the making-ready-to-sail, the retrieval of the thing that held you. The confusion with 'dropping anchor' is so common that the phrase now carries the wrong meaning in most people's minds.
usage: They weighed anchor before dawn, the capstan grinding in the dark, and were through the heads before the town was awake.

definition: Fate; destiny; one's appointed lot; the Weird Sisters who weave the thread of a life. Used as a noun before it became an adjective.
lost: Weird began as a noun — a thing, a force, a fact — and became an adjective meaning strange. The original sense was that fate felt uncanny; we kept the feeling and lost the thing that caused it.
usage: He had a sense of his weird from early on; not knowledge exactly, but the feeling of a path already laid.

definition: The sky, the vault of heaven — but understood as a dome with weight and shape, not merely the absence of ground. The welkin has a ceiling; the sky does not.
lost: "Sky" is descriptive. Welkin carries the sense of a vault — something arched above you with intent.
usage: The birds rose until the welkin swallowed them whole.

definition: An exclamation of grief or lamentation; alas — but with more body than alas, more duration, as if the word itself is a sound wrung from the chest.
lost: Alas is literary; wellaway is vocal. The word was a cry before it was a word, and it kept that quality — saying wellaway meant producing a sound, not writing one.
usage: Wellaway, she said, and meant it entirely, and sat down in the road where she was and did not get up for some time.

definition: World-pain; the depression that comes from comparing the world as it is with the world as it should be — borrowed from German because English has no equivalent.
lost: Named the specific grief of idealism disappointed — Weltschmerz is not the pain of personal loss but the pain of a world that doesn't measure up to what you believed it could be, which requires its own word because it is its own condition.
usage: The Weltschmerz had settled in sometime during his thirties and showed no signs of lifting, which he had come to regard as a reasonable response to the available evidence.

definition: A cyst or benign tumour under the skin; a blemish that grows slowly and patiently and refuses to be ignored.
lost: Named the body's quiet insurgency — a wen is not an illness but an inconvenience that has taken structural form, the body producing something it did not intend.
usage: He had a wen on his neck that had been there so long it had become part of how people recognised him.

definition: Blood-price; the monetary compensation owed to a family for the killing of one of their kin.
lost: Encoded the radical idea that a life has a calculable social value — not sentimental, but precise and public.
usage: The wergild was paid in silver over three years, and after the third payment, the feud was formally ended.

definition: Formerly; once upon a time; at some past period now remote enough to feel like another world.
lost: Formerly is a date; whilom is a distance. The word understood that some things in the past are not merely earlier but genuinely elsewhere — another world that happened to be this one.
usage: The whilom greatness of the building was visible in the bones of it, in the height of the ceilings and the quality of the ironwork, which the rest had not maintained.

definition: Attractive and appealing; charming in a way that draws people toward rather than merely impressing them.
lost: Named beauty that welcomes — winsome is the opposite of beauty that intimidates, and that distinction no longer has a word.
usage: There was something winsome about his confusion that made everyone in the room want to help him.

definition: Having a longing, pensive sadness; yearning for something past, absent, or unattainable; the mood of looking at what cannot be reached.
lost: Named the gaze directed at something beyond getting — wistful is sadness with a specific object, loss that knows exactly what it has lost and cannot stop looking at it.
usage: She gave the house a wistful look as the taxi pulled away, which was the last time she saw it.

definition: In addition to all this; nevertheless; at the same time — a hinge word that holds contradictions in a single breath without resolving them.
lost: "But" forces a choice. Withal holds the tension open and lets it stand.
usage: She was exhausted and, withal, unwilling to stop while daylight remained.

definition: The ridge between the shoulder blades of a horse; the highest point of the back, where the neck meets the spine.
lost: Named the anatomical point by which horses are measured — the wither is the fulcrum of the horse's power, where the neck's leverage meets the back's strength.
usage: He measured the horse at the withers before he named a price, which took about four seconds and told him everything he needed.

definition: Within; inside; interior — the emphatic form of 'within' that insists on the interiority it names.
lost: Named inside with a deliberateness that 'within' has lost — withinside is inside asserted, inside made a point of, the interior that knows itself to be interior.
usage: Withinside the walls, the silence was complete; outside, the city continued its business without interest in what the walls contained.

definition: Mad; insane; wild with passion or fury — the Old English adjective that named mental disorder before lunacy, madness, or derangement arrived to replace it.
lost: Named madness as a state of wildness rather than illness — to be wood was to have gone beyond reason into something animal and dangerous, which is a different understanding of mental disorder than the clinical vocabulary that replaced it.
usage: The storm had driven them all a little wood by the third day; by the fourth, no one was surprised by anything anyone did.

definition: The ghost of a person; an apparition seen shortly before or after death; a pale, insubstantial figure on the edge of visibility.
lost: Named the ghost at its most attenuated — a wraith is barely there, barely visible, barely committed to existing, which distinguishes it from the full-presence ghost.
usage: She moved through the house like a wraith those first weeks, not quite present, not quite absent.

definition: To blow with a rushing, blustering sound; the noise of wind around buildings or through trees.
lost: Named wind by the sound it makes on arrival — wuther is what wind does to architecture, the voice of weather finding gaps.
usage: The wind wuthered around the eaves all night and made sleep difficult and waking uncertain.

definition: Fate as an active, weaving force; destiny not as destination but as process.
lost: Fate implies a fixed end point — wyrd implied something woven continuously, which is a far more unsettling idea.
usage: He had stopped fighting his wyrd by the time the messenger arrived.

definition: A dragon with two legs and wings rather than four legs; a heraldic beast; the lesser dragon of the English imagination.
lost: Named the specific heraldic creature that is not quite a dragon — a wyvern has two legs where a dragon has four, which matters in heraldry and in the taxonomy of things that don't exist but need to be precisely described.
usage: The crest showed a wyvern rampant on a field of blue, which was either a statement of territorial ambition or the eccentric choice of an ancestor nobody could now consult.

definition: Hospitable to strangers; welcoming of the unknown guest.
lost: Named a virtue the modern world has largely stopped aspiring to — xenodochial hospitality assumes the stranger deserves welcome before they've earned it.
usage: The village had a xenodochial tradition older than its church: the road through meant the gate was open.

definition: Ready; nimble; quick and easily handled — of a vessel: quick to the helm, responsive. Of a person: alert and prepared.
lost: Ready is passive; yare is active. To be yare is to be already in motion toward what is needed, not merely available for it.
usage: The boat was yare in a way that larger vessels couldn't manage, answering the helm before the helmsman had finished the thought.

definition: Went; went away; the archaic past tense of 'to go' — a verb that became so irregular it disappeared, leaving 'went' (from 'wend') to do double duty.
lost: Named going with an Old English directness — yede was the simple past of motion, before the language borrowed 'went' from 'wend' and let yede fall silent. The word is gone; the act remains.
usage: He yede before dawn without word to anyone, and was not seen in that part of the country again.

definition: Long ago; in the distant past; the time before living memory, when things were different in ways no one can now verify.
lost: Long ago is neutral; yore carries a tonality — days of yore were understood to be both better and irrecoverable, which is a specific relationship to the past.
usage: Of yore this had been common land, and some of the older inhabitants still walked across it as if the enclosure had never happened.
